title: Code Overview: MATLAB to Python Port
text:
Python port (planned)
The port will implement the key algorithms using:

NumPy/SciPy for FFT-based cross-correlation
OpenCV for image processing (edge detection, dilation, erosion, hole-filling)
Matplotlib/Plotly for interactive visualisation


Key algorithms to port

2D cross-correlation via FFT — the core DIC algorithm
Colour-based marker detection — exclusive colour plane extraction
Marker identification pipeline — edge detection, dilation, hole-filling, erosion, restoration
Weighted centroid calculation — marker position determination
Rigid body compensation — separating camera motion from object displacement
Calibration — polynomial fit for angle estimation

# Placeholder — Python implementation coming soon
import numpy as np
from scipy.fft import fft2, ifft2, fftshift

def cross_correlate_2d(image1, image2):
    """
    2D cross-correlation using FFT.
    Finds the displacement between two images.
    """
    f1 = fft2(image1)
    f2 = fft2(image2)
    cc = ifft2(f1 * np.conj(f2))
    cc_shifted = fftshift(np.abs(cc))

    # Find peak location
    peak = np.unravel_index(np.argmax(cc_shifted), cc_shifted.shape)

    # Convert to displacement
    dy = peak[0] - image1.shape[0] // 2
    dx = peak[1] - image1.shape[1] // 2

    return dx, dy, cc_shifted


Interactive demonstrations (planned)
Once the port is complete, this page will include:

Live cross-correlation demo — upload or select test images, see the FFT correlation surface
Marker detection walkthrough — step through the 7-stage pipeline with intermediate results
Calibration explorer — interactive plot of angle calibration curves

title: Discussion and Conclusion
text:
4
5 Discussion And Conclusion
Discussion
A great number of areas of investigation and research fell within the acceptable limits of the project specification. Within the project, a large number were tackled, and to varying degrees of depth. A large element of this project is speculative in nature: An exploration of the viability of various approaches in solving a breadth of possible scenarios. 
The project specification was interpreted in certain ways in order to create a more specific form of problem that could be more rigorously analysed, investigated and solved. In doing this, not all possible interpretations of the specification were investigated.
Here are a number of possible future areas of investigation:
The ability to locate and track a number of objects within a bounded area: Within the course of this project, only one ‘critical’ (important and movable) object was considered to be within the bounded area. What if more critical objects were in the area? How would a device discriminate between them?
Object detection based on shape rather than colour: In a crude way, this was used. To be reproduced in the mask, an object had to of a sufficient size, as otherwise it would be eroded away in the process described in section 2.3.3. Very small markers could not be used. What if the device were able to identify a pattern of features on an object, and choose to retain the object based on this identification? This pattern or feature recognition could be achieved, to some degree, using DIC techniques, as these involve the matching of features.
Identification of displacement of object markers when of a non-specific colour: If only one object moves within a specific area, then a DIC analysis of that area will, once any rigid-body disparity has been compensated for, show some peak at the disparity value. The larger the area compared to the object, the more the object disparity is in danger of being ‘drowned out’ by the rigid body disparity (or lack of). Thus techniques may have to be developed to zoom in on likely areas of object displacement, in order the object disparity values be more distinct.
Use of DIC to determine plane angle and depth [9] [10]: This approach was considered in the course of the project. Comparing the relative positions of common features on a pair of images provides a potential method for determining the depth of different parts of the image to using markers, and, obviously, does not require the same prior knowledge of the object plane, or preparation of surface (placement of markers etc.). It does require that the surface has certain properties though; for instance, it should have a distinct amorphous pattern on it, otherwise features will be hard to identify, or areas of regional correlation may be erroneously identified as significant. Also, the kind of warping that occurs on a set of features within an image, in addition to their displacement due to depth, would also have to be investigated. 
The use and benefits of a multiple camera system: Human vision makes use of two image sources (eyes) to determine depth information, and to provide contingency should one fail. However, the advantages of a multiple-camera system should outweigh the increased cost relative to a single vision system. Original plans for prototype systems considered the use of two cameras, and the reduction of the material requirements of the system to a single camera represents a significant improvement.
The use of a self-lit system: As was seen in section 4.3.3, the lighting affects the ability for the system to function. A future system may attempt to reduce the effects of ambient lighting by using its own light system.
The ability to recognise object displacement on a curved or variable surface: The system, as it presently exists, is able to locate a number of boundary markers and infer that they exist as fixed and known points on a flat surface. If the system were able to recognise boundary markers, and infer from these the position of an object on a curved or variable surface (such as a portion of a sphere or spheroid shape), then the number of scenarios the system would be able to tackle, and the accuracy of the system, would increase. A system capable or disseminating object position on a curved surface would, for instance, be able to compensate for the curvature and warping of an image caused by a camera lens, for instance. 
The implementation of DIC sub-pixel algorithms: Presently, the accuracy of the system is limited, in part, by what are effectively the pixel resolution figures (the number of mm2 per pixel). Sub-pixel DIC algorithms have been developed by the university to allow the accuracy of a DIC system to exceed the camera resolution. 
The creation of a system to recognise displacement of an object in three dimensions: This is touched upon briefly in section 1.2.3. Viewing an object from more than one perspective allows a system to gauge displacement in three dimensions. This is an example of a potential benefit of a multiple camera system.  
5.2 Conclusion
Using a single, specific test problem, with a specific boundary size, marker colour, object colour, rigid body displacement and object displacement, the device was able to identify the real-life displacement of the object to a high degree of accuracy. Thus, the system could be said to work.
In the main test example, the device was able to accurately recognise the object disparity to within an area less than 0.01 per cent of the bounded object area (See section 4.2.4.4).  
A number of other examples provided a slightly more rigorous testing of various stages of the system. These revealed the effects differing conditions have on the device, and suggested ways they may be used and/or compensated for.
The device was able to identify, in normal conditions, both objects and markers, and to extrapolate useful information from these. It also allows the potential to extrapolate further information, including information on distance of object plane from camera, and ‘vertical plane angle:’ the angle the camera meets with the line normal to the face of the object plane, in the vertical plane normal to the object plane. 
The device operated using a single camera system for a range of tasks, including both device positioning and object positioning. Original designs considered used two cameras rather than just one, and the single-camera system used represents a significant improvement on these ideas due to the reduced material cost.
Due to the breadth of areas involved in reaching a finished and functioning prototype, some areas have been demonstrated in a fairly anecdotal fashion, but those that have been tested comprehensively (For example, section 4.4) have yielded results that have been self-consistent, and allowed the desired outcome to be achieved.

title: Theory

Theory****
2.1 Section introduction
This section details the theory behind the two different types of technique used within the project, digital image correlation (DIC) and fiduciary marker analysis.
2.2 Digital Image Correlation
2.2.1 Introduction******
Digital Image Correlation techniques are those that use computers to compare two or more images for common features. They are often based on fast Fourier transforms (FFTs) which are themselves a computer-optimised form of discrete Fourier transforms (DFTs).
2.2.2 Cross-correlation using Fourier Transforms
2.2.2.1 Basic Principles******
Fourier transforms convert a signal whose magnitude varies with an independent variable (such as time or position) into a frequency-dependent signal [1] [2]: equation [Eqn. 2.2.1]
The inverse Fourier transform is: equation [Eqn. 2.2.2]**
such that: equation [Eqn. 2.2.3]**
A cross-correlation signal can be formed using the equation: equation [Eqn. 2.2.4]**
where: equation [Eqn. 2.2.5]**
Two important properties of Fourier transforms need to be noted: equation [Eqn. 2.2.6]** equation [Eqn. 2.2.7]**
thus: equation [Eqn. 2.2.8]**
If s2(x) = s1(x – dx) (– i.e. one signal is the same as the other, but translated by a quantity dx), then:
[Eqn. 2.2.9]
Thus the output function cc(x) includes the displacement between the two signals.
For impulse functions, this result is useful. 
2.2.2.2 Basic Example
If: equation [Eqn. 2.2.10] equation [Eqn. 2.2.11]**
i.e. both s1 and s2 are impulse functions translated to a position +x1, s2 being s1 further displaced by dx, then: equation equation equation [Eqn. 2.2.12]**

[Fig. 2.2.2]
2.2.2.3 Multiple term example******
Consider the case of [3.2.9], where: equation [Eqn. 2.2.13]**
and: equation (where k is a real constant)
[Eqn. 2.2.14]
i.e. Both signals comprise of two impulse functions, but shifted by dx. equation equation [Eqn. 2.2.15]**
Representing this graphically:
If s1(x) is: [Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]

[Fig. 2.2.3]
then the output signal, cc(x), is: [Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]
Output signal
[Fig. 2.2.4]**
Often, the equation component of the function has a significantly greater amplitude than the others, and so can be identified as representing the correct displacement between the two input signals. In the above example, finding the mean x position of all signal components, or filtering out any components whose amplitude is below a certain threshold of the maximum, would both produce the correct displacement dx. 
2.2.3 Application Of Technique To Image Processing
An array is a series of (N by 1) elements: discrete values that vary depending on their position within the array. Thus, an array could be considered a signal, comprising of a finite series of equally spaced impulse functions, each multiplied by different position-dependent amplitudes. equation [Eqn. 2.2.18]**
(where k is the distance between components.)
If: equation [Eqn. 2.2.19]**
then: equation [Eqn. 2.2.20]**
An array of this form could also be represented as a sequence of coloured blocks, whose colour (or intensity of colour) is dependent upon the value of the array element: [Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]
Two ways of representing the contents of a one-dimensional array: As a set of equally spaced impulse functions of variable value, and as a set of coloured blocks, whose colour depends upon the element value
[Fig. 2.2.5]
Similarly, a two-dimensional matrix (M by N elements) could be represented as a two dimensional block of M by N value-dependent pixels: [Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]
Two-dimensional graphical equivalent of the above method for representing the contents of a matrix
[Fig. 2.2.6]**
This is how a greyscale image is stored. (In an actual greyscale image, very light colours usually represent the highest pixel values, and very dark colours represent very low pixel values.)
Thus, a two-dimensional version of the cross-correlation function shown before can be used on a pair of equally sized images to determine correlation between them. 
2.2.4 An Example:
s1 and s2 are two greyscale images, 64 pixels by 64 pixels. 
s2 is s1, shifted by (2, -5) pixels.
Input signals s1(x,y) and s2(x,y)
[Fig. 3.2.7]
The cross-correlation is:
Output signal cc(x,y)
[Fig. 3.2.8]
This can also be represented in a normalised form, using: equation [Eqn. 3.2.9]
where max{f(x,y)} is the greatest value of any element within the array.
This gives:
Normalised output signal ncc(x,y)
[Fig. 3.2.10]
Normalising the cross-correlation allows standardised analysis to be performed on the resulting function. As the un-normalised cross-correlation represents the sum of all pixels that can be cross-correlated at each position, the actual value of the peak depends upon, for instance, image size (which affects the number of pixels summed to form the peak) and average intensity. Normalising allows standard functions (such as one that removes all values below 80% of the maximum – and thus those that are unlikely to include the image disparity position) to be applied to the code irrespective of the properties of the original input signals.
As you can see, the intensity peak exists at the position of the displacement between the two images, and can be identified to find the x- and y-axis disparity between the two images.
2.3 Fiduciary Markers
2.3.1 Introduction******
The other broad area involved in this project is fiduciary marker identification and analysis. Fiduciary markers are distinguishable parts of a physical body that can be used to help determine positions of real features from images.  
For fiduciary markers to be useful, they need to be easily identifiable, and something about how the markers represent parts of the physical body (such as real-life distance between two markers), needs to be known.
2.3.2 Marker Identification By Colour
For a computer to recognise a marker in an image, something about the visual properties of the marker needs to be identifiable, by the computer, from those of the rest of the image. One of the most common approaches is to make fiduciary markers of a distinct colour, not naturally present on the object of interest.
Obviously, this involves using colour images, rather than greyscale images. A bitmap (with a ‘.bmp’ file extension) image file stores a colour image M by N pixels in size as a M by N by 3 matrix[3]. Each of the three positions of the third dimension stores the red, green and blue component (respectively) of the colour image.
For example, if the colour image is:
Test colour image
[Fig. 2.3.1]**
then it will be stored as three separate intensity planes, representing the red, green and blue components of the complete image
Red, green and blue components of previous image
[Fig. 2.3.2]
Similarly, for a colour image taken using a webcam, stored as a bitmap image:
Colour image taken using a simple webcam
[Fig. 2.3.3]
The image is stored as the following three intensity planes:
Red Green
Blue**
Red, green and blue components of the colour webcam image
[Fig. 2.3.4]
Different combinations of the three stored colour intensity planes produce other intensity planes whose intensity is greatest only at specific colours. For example, a red colour has a high intensity value on the red intensity plane, but a white area also has a high intensity value on the red intensity plane (as white light is a combination of all colours of visible light). By subtracting the average (greyscale) intensity of all three planes from the red intensity plane, a resulting intensity plane is created where the greatest intensity values occur only at parts of the image that are exclusively red; thus it takes information from all three colour planes to determine areas of a specific colour:
Exclusive* red intensity planes for both test images: Note the increased contrast compared with the non-exclusive red intensity images, which would make identifying markers of the specific colour easier.*
[Fig. 2.3.5]
This kind of processing is useful in determining marker positions based on colour. A first stage in identifying a marker could be to find the areas of greatest intensity in the exclusive intensity plane of the colour of the marker, although more processing needs to take place to make sure the marker is correctly identified.
A fiduciary marker need not be of a red, green or blue colour, as any colour can be identified using the right combination of red, green and blue intensity planes. Even black can be used- by creating a marker intensity plane that is the inverse of the greyscale intensity plane (itself the average of the three colour intensity planes) – although using a marker on the greyscale is not advisable in real-life situations, as both very bright and very dark areas of images can be the result of lighting, rather than object colour.
A simple example demonstrating the problems with using black or white as marker colours: It would be difficult for a computer to identify the headphones from the surrounding area as the shadows formed nearby are of a similar colour. This would not be the case if the headphones were, for instance, yellow.
[Fig. 2.3.6]
2.3.3 Further Marker Identification Processes
For a marker to be useful, it should ideally be identifiable in a Boolean sense: A particular point of an image should either 1) contain the marker, or 2) not contain the marker. There should be no ambiguity as to where the marker exists within the image. In practice, this means that the computer should be able to create a 1-bit mask of the marker from the original image:
Creating a marker mask: The computer should be able to identify the marker in the image (left), discriminating it from other image features, and create a binary mask of it (right)
[Fig. 2.3.7]
The process for doing this is as follows:
From 2.3.2: Create an intensity plane that distinguishes the marker from other features using an appropriate combination of the red, green and blue intensity planes of the colour image [4]. In Fig. 3.3.7, this was done by:
inverting the average of the green and blue planes:
Inverted average of green and blue planes**
[Fig. 2.3.8a]**
(II.) inverting the red plane:
Inverted red plane
[Fig. 2.3.8b]**
(III.) subtracting (II.) from (I):
– (II)
[Fig. 2.3.8c]
Subtract the mean value of the image (its DC component) from itself, then     
 normalise, to increase contrast. (Repeat more than once if necessary) 
DC subtracted and normalised once
[Fig. 2.3.9a]
DC subtracted and normalised twice
[Fig. 2.3.9b]
Apply an edge-finding filter to the image: This essentially works by creating a blank mask (all 0s) of the original image. It then sets a pixel at a particular position within the mask, if there is a significant gradient change between neighbouring pixels of the corresponding position in the intensity plane (original) image. There are a number of methods for determining whether a particular point on an image represents the edge of an object. The Sobel method is the standard one used in Matlab for edge-detection [5]. 
1-bit edge mask of intensity plane
[Fig. 2.3.10]

Dilate the mask: If a pixel in the mask is set to 1, then the pixels adjacent to it are also set to 1. This effectively thickens any lines created by the edge detector [6]. 

Dilated mask
[Fig. 2.3.11]

Fill any holes: All unset pixels that exist within a closed boundary of set pixels are themselves set. This effectively fills any ‘holes’ existing in the closed bounds of a detected edge. As the edge filter is designed to identify possible edges of distinct objects, filling the areas in between these boundaries should fill in the part of the image where the marker exists.

 
Filled mask
[Fig. 2.3.12]

Erode the image: Any set pixels adjacent to unset pixels are themselves unset. This effectively erodes all objects (groups of set pixels) in the mask. If an object is small enough, the process will remove it completely, leaving only larger, more significant objects that are less likely to be due to visual noise, and more likely to be the marker object. If a single application of the erosion process does not remove all erroneous objects, it may be applied a second time (As is the case in the particular code used). 

Mask eroded once: Note a small erroneous object is still present
[Fig. 2.3.13a]
Mask eroded twice: Now the small erroneous object has been removed, but the remaining object has been significantly reduced in size.
[Fig. 2.3.13b]

Restore the marker object: The mask created in the previous stage [Fig. 3.3.12b] contains only one object. This exists at the same position as the marker object, but is smaller, and of a slightly different shape (due to the arbitrary nature of the erosion process) than the actual marker object within the original image. The location of this eroded object can, though, be used to determine which of the objects in the un-eroded mask [Fig. 3.3.11] is the marker object. The location of the eroded object can be determined, and the object that exists at this location in the un-eroded mask can be located. All other objects can be removed, leaving a mask containing only the marker object, as desired [7].

Marker mask: This result is the right image in [Fig. 3.3.7]
[Fig. 2.3.14]**
2.3.4 Marker Position Determination
The final stage is to find the position of the centre of the marker. This is necessary when comparing the positions of a number of markers to determine information about, for example, plane location and camera position.
The process is as follows:
Using the same mask example as before: [Fig. 3.3.13]**
Example marker mask
[Fig. 2.3.15]
The marker object is located and loaded into a separate 1-bit matrix:
Marker object isolated within rectangle
[Fig. 2.3.16]**
The row and column coordinates that mark the start of the marker object rectangle (i.e. the coordinates that give the distance from the origin of [Fig. 3.3.14], to the origin of [Fig. 3.3.15]) are also stored.
To find the centre of the marker object in [Fig. 3.3.15], a weighted average method is used for both the x-position and y-position.
To calculate the x-position, the following formula [8] is used: equation [Eqn. *2.3.1]***
where: equation The analogous operation, using y-positions and summations of row elements, is used to find equation : the centre of the object in the y-axis.
These are added to the start position of the rectangle, to find the position of the centre of the object in the image. equation

title: Results
text:
4
 Results
4.1 Section Introduction
This section provides results attained through the use and creation of the prototype system. It is split into three sub-sections: 
Results of all processes to a single test example.
Results of various processes to other example inputs.
Results required for machine calibration and testing system stability.
Results Of System Using Single Test Case
Introduction
Here, the stages of the prototype system listed in section 3 are demonstrated using a single test case.
In the test case, the bounded area was a rectangle 70mm wide and 50 mm high. This was marked out using four black square blocks 10 mm wide. Black was used as the marker colour as it is a poor choice of marker colour (due to the reasons given in section 2.3.2), and so demonstrating that the system works with this colour scheme suggest that it should also work with others (where the affect of bright lights and shadows can be more easily mitigated).
The object is represented by a small movable red rectangle 8mm wide and 6 mm high. Am image of this object within the bounded area, at the ‘correct’ position, is taken, and loaded, as an archived image, into the system at the start of the program sequence:
Archive image of marker object (red rectangle) in correct position
[Fig. 4.2.1]
Note that, within the bounded area, other objects, of a different colour, also exist. To work, the prototype system should not be affected by the position of these other objects. The background is graph paper, which, as a repeating pattern, creates ‘noise’ when a DIC approach is used (due to localised areas of image correlation). Both of these factors make the test case good for demonstrating the rigorousness of the system.
The geometry of the markers is also entered into the system at the start. The position of the four markers, from each other and in millimetres, is loaded into the system as the following 4 x 2 element array:



0
0




70

Marker position matrix used
[Table. 4.2.1]
Where the first column gives the horizontal distance from the origin, and the second gives the vertical distance from the origin.
Also given are information on the colour combination to use to find both the object marker (red), and the boundary markers (black). In the case of the boundary marker, this would be an instruction to convert the colour image into a greyscale image (by combining the pixel average of the three colour intensity plane) and then inverting, so dark areas become areas of high intensity. In the case of the red object marker, the procedure shown in section 2.3.3 is used. 
The physical apparatus used for the test is as follows: A webcam is fixed to a certain position on ruler. The head of the webcam is fixed, vertically aligned with reference to its base, and facing forwards in the direction parallel to that of the ruler. At the end of the ruler a small cocktail is attached, propagating from the centre of the ruler width a small distance beyond that of the ruler. This provides a thin fulcrum about which the camera may rotate in the horizontal plane normal to the object plane, which is one of the walls of the room. 
Image of the camera apparatus used
[Fig. 4.2.2]**
The ruler is rested on the top of a table that is horizontally balanced with the markers on the object plane, and at a height such that the camera image is at the same height as the bounded area of the object plane. This reduces the number of positional factors that need to be considered, and that the effect of a single variable can be more easily isolated. 
Stage 1a: Finding Rotation Parallel To Plane
Determining Centres Of Markers
If, to begin with, the camera image is at both the wrong angle in the plane parallel to the image, and horizontally perpendicular to it, then its position can be corrected one stage at time. To begin with, the angle of rotation parallel to the image plane is compensated found.
In the test example, the first image entered into the prototype system was as shown below on the left. The prototype first identified the boundary markers as shown below on the right:
The first camera image (left) and the marker mask of the image (right)
[Fig. 4.2.3]
Four sub-masks are then formed from this mask, effectively splitting the mask up into four quarters. The sub-masks are adjusted such that the corner of the quarter is at the origin of the sub-mask (e.g. for the bottom right quarter, the sub-image is flipped horizontally and vertically; for the top right quarter, the image is just flipped horizontally).
The co-ordinates of the centres of mass (and thus the central points of each marker) of each quarter are then located using the method described in section 2.3.4, giving the co-ordinates of the centre of the image from the corner of the image it is closest to:

Sub-masks of the (a) top left, (b) top right, (c) bottom left, and (d) bottom right quarters of the mask image

Determining Rotation
It is now relatively simple to determine the plane-parallel rotation of the camera to the object plane. The process is as follows:
Compare the vertical distance between the two points on the left (Left half of image), with the two points on the right (Right half of image). The camera is closer to the side whose points are further apart. In the test case, the two points on the left are further apart.
The points from this half are more accurate representations of the centres of the markers, as the camera is closer to them, and thus their effective resolution in the image is higher. Due to the way the markers are formed, borderlines of a certain number of pixels are created which extend slightly beyond the boundaries of the marker in the original image (Fig. 2.3.10; Fig 2.3.11). The distortion of the marker shape and position due to these borderlines are greater if the marker is smaller; thus the larger (in this case closer) markers provide more accurate estimates of their central positions. These two position co-ordinates on the left side are considered thus those used to determine the angle.
The horizontal displacement between the two points is divided by the vertical displacement, and the tangent of this value if found to determine the angle between the two markers.
If the angle is small (less than one degree in magnitude, in the case of the prototype), then the program passes to the next stage. Otherwise, the angle between the two points is given to the user, together with instructions on what action to take. In the test case, the first input image yields and angle of –6.1 degrees (due to the way positive and negative displacement is defined, and the instruction: 
Rotate Camera in parallel with plane:
6.1
degrees anticlockwise
is given to the user.
The prototype now waits for the user to reposition the camera, and take a second image. When the user indicates, the prototype loads in the new image, and performs the same analysis on this as it did on the first. In the test case, the new image was:
Second (position adjusted) image
[Fig. 4.2.6]**
This time, the plane-parallel angle was within the one degree acceptable limit (Calculated as –0.3 degrees), yielding the following output:
Rotational Angle OK
The program now passes the image to the next stage.
Stage 1b: Finding Horizontal Rotation Normal To Plane
The next stage is to correct the angle of the camera in the horizontal plane normal to the object plane. The process is as follows:
A mask of the image is created:
Mask of second image
[Fig. 4.2.7]**
and the centres of each of the markers are found using the method detailed in section 4.2.2.1. 
The vertical distances between the top left and the bottom left, and the top right and bottom right, marker points are found, and the ratio between them is found. In the test case, the positions of the centres of the four points, with respect to the top left point, are:

[Table. 4.2.4]**
The left side vertical value is thus: 131.0 – 0 = 131.0
  And the right side vertical value is: 126.2 – 9.7 = 116.5
** The ratio of right over left is: 116.5  = 0.89********
                    **131.0******
Coefficients for a third-degree polynomial are then loaded into the system that allow it to infer a horizontal rotation angle from this ratio. The details for how this is done are given in section 4.4. In the test case, a ratio of 0.89 represents an angle of -16.7 degrees, where the minus sign means the angle is to the left of the normal of the image direction and object plane, in the horizontal plane normal to the object plane:
Diagram showing meaning of positive and negative angle values
[Fig. 4.2.8]
The real angle of the images was – to within around 2 degrees of accuracy – 17 degrees, so the estimated value is valid. The prototype determines whether  the angle of the camera is acceptable or not. As before, a tolerance value is chosen- this time five degrees- and, clearly, 17 degrees lies outside this tolerance. Thus the code informs the user how far the camera has to be rotated in order for it to be correctly positioned, waits for the user to reposition it and take a new image, and loads the new image.
With the new image, the process repeats again. With the test case, input image, and the mask of the image, are as follows: 
(a) (b)
The third input image (a), and its marker mask (b)
[Fig. 4.2.9]
This gives marker positions as follows:

New relative positions of marker centres
[Table. 4.2.4]
Which gives a ratio of 1.00, which equates to an angle of 1.09 degrees; acceptable for passing to the next stage.
Stage 2: Finding Object Disparity
Introduction
Now the camera has been positioned correctly, the prototype needs to determine how far the object (the red rectangle) is displaced from its position in the archive image. In all current (non-archive) images, the object is 6.0mm further to the right, and 4.0mm lower, than it is in the archive image.
In order to determine the disparity of the object, any disparity between the test and archive images as a whole – known as rigid body disparity – needs to be determined. Stage one of the process allows the angle of the camera to be corrected relative to the plane, but does not correct the position along the axes of the plane from which the camera views the bounded area. 
Once this rigid body disparity has been found, it can be compensated for when performing a digital image correlation (DIC), so that ‘like’ regions – regions of the images common to both archive and current – can be compared.
An object disparity value found by performing a DIC on the common regions of the pair of images, in pixels, then needs to be converted to a value in millimetres, by using a scale found by comparing real-life boundary marker geometries with those located in pixels.
Thus, stage two essentially involves three sub-stages, each of which will now be detailed for the test case in sequence.
Sub-stage 1: Rigid-body Disparity
Rigid body disparity, the disparity between two images due to a shift between the images as a whole (rather than displacement of a small-sub region of the image – i.e. the movable object), can be determined in a number of ways: 
If the images are of a flat surface with an amorphous (non-repeating) pattern on it – such as wood-chip wallpaper –, and where any movable objects within the image areas occupy only a small image area, and/or of a low intensity within the intensity plane used, then simply performing a DIC of both whole images should produce an output with the greatest intensity peak at the rigid-body displacement position.
There are a number of problems with this approach: A DIC of the whole image will be affected by both rigid body and object disparity, and may produce a results whose peak is at a position that is a ‘compromise’ between the rigid-body displacement (which should have the greatest ‘energy’ in the image, as most of the features of the image match at this disparity position), and the object displacement. Thus it would produce the wrong value for rigid-body disparity.
Also, most machine work surfaces do not have fixed amorphous patterns on them in a region of interest. Consider a work surface on a drilling machine: Originally, its work surface may be shiny and featureless; over time features on the surface may result in the form of scratches and general wear, but these features are likely to change over time - as more scratches are introduced -, and so cannot be used to determine rigid body disparity between two images taken – for instance – months apart. 
A better method, therefore, may be to use the boundary markers found in the previous stages. This should be more reliable, as they are strong, already identified features, which are not as subject to change in appearance over time than the region actually within the working area.
Thus, in the test case, the rigid-body disparity is found by comparing the boundary marker masks of the current and archive image, rather than the images themselves. 
The image and mask for both the current image and the archive image are as follows:
(a) (b)
The archive image (a) and the current image (b): Note that the lighting is significantly different for both images, but this does not affect the ability for the computer to identify the boundary markers
[Fig. 4.2.10]**
(a) (b)
The archive mask (a) and the current mask (b): The differences in lighting are compensated for
[Fig. 4.2.11]
This produces the following normalised cross-correlation image:
Normalised cross-correlation of the two masks: Note the clarity of the main intensity peak
[Fig. 4.2.12]
The position of the peak is identified at x = -20 pixels, and y = 1 pixel. 
This is the rigid body disparity in pixels.
Sub-stage 2: Object Disparity
Having identified the rigid body disparity, one now needs to identify the displacement of the object within the plane, rather than in the images.
The object is located (almost) exclusively in both images by using the technique given in section 2.3.3, giving the following pair of intensity planes:
(a) (b)**
Object colour intensity plane images from (a) the archive image and (b) the current image
[Fig. 4.2.13]
The displacement between the objects in these two images could be considered to the sum of the object disparity, and the rigid body disparity. One could simply determine this disparity, and subtract the rigid body disparity from it to obtain the object disparity; or one could use the rigid body information to form rigid-body compensated object images. These images show only the regions of the larger images common to both images:
(a) (b)**
Rigid-body compensated object intensity planes: These images now show only the regions of the original images common to both; rigid-body disparity has been removed 
[Fig. 4.2.14]
These images are 20 pixels (the x-axis RB disparity) smaller in width, and 1 pixel (the y-axis RB disparity) smaller in height. As they are smaller, the region containing the object represents a larger proportion of the total image area, and thus has a stronger presence within the image, and produces a slightly more pronounced peak when DIC is used on it:
NCC of the RB compensated object images 
[Fig. 4.2.15]
The position of the peak on this NCC graph (Fig. 4.2.15) is x = 15 pixels, and y = 10 pixels.
This is the recorded value of the object disparity.
One can consider how accurate these values seem by comparing them with the actual object displacement: 6.0mm to the right, and 4.0mm downward. Due to the way Matlab stores images, the origin is taken as the top left corner, thus displacements to the right, and downward, are both positive displacements. Both the x and y object displacement values are positive, which means the recorded object displacement is in the correct direction. 
The ratio between x displacement and y displacement in both cases (actual in mm, and recorded in pixels) is 3/2 (15pixels/10pixels = 6mm/4mm = 3/2), again suggesting that the displacement value found is accurate.
Sub-stage 3: Real-life Disparity
The final stage involves converting the pixel disparities into millimetre disparities, by finding the ratio between pixels and millimetres.
The locations of the marker centres on the current image are as follows:

Positions of marker centres – in pixels
[Table. 4.2.5]**
The position of the two horizontal edges – Top right (x) less top left (x), and bottom right (x) less bottom left (x) – are not exactly the same, so a mean is used, to provide an average horizontal edge length in pixels. The same is done with the two vertical edges, giving a horizontal edge value of 190.5 pixels, and a vertical edge value of 123.7 pixels.
From the data provided at the start on real geometry, the real vertical edge distance is 70.0mm, and the vertical edge distance is 50.0mm. Dividing real edge distance by pixel edge distances gives a vertical ratio of 0.40 mm per pixel, and a horizontal ratio of 0.37 mm per pixel. These figures could be thought of as representing the system resolution, without resorting to sub-pixel algorithms.
Multiplying the pixel disparities by these ratios gives real-life disparity estimates of 5.5mm (actual: 6.0mm) horizontally, and 4.0mm (actual: 4.0mm) vertically. This gives an overall magnitude of displacement of 6.8mm (actual 7.2mm). Thus the disparity values are accurate to within half a millimetre of the true value, in both the horizontal and vertical axes.
The bounded object plane area was 70.0mm x 50.0mm, or 3500.0mm2; the object displacement figure found was accurate to ±0.5mm2 (The square root of 0.52 + 0.02). This gives a calculated accuracy level to within 0.5 / 3500, or 0.14 per mil. Using the 0.40 and 0.37 mm / pixel resolution figures calculated earlier gives a displacement determination accuracy of ±0.54mm2, the same value to 1 significant figure. 
Other Examples
Introduction
Though the example given in the previous section shows how the processes work, it does not show how the processes operate in other conditions.
Effect Of Non-critical Objects On Identification Of Critical Objects
In the prototype, two types of object need to be identified: those that mark the boundaries of the area of interest, and the movable object of interest. These are identified by colour. Below, three examples are given where camera position, and movable object of interest are fixed, but other objects – of a non-specified colour – differ in position:
Test Image 1 Marker Mask Object Mask
[Fig. 4.3.1]**
Test Image 2 Marker Mask Object Mask
[Fig. 4.3.2]**
Test Image 3 Marker Mask Object Mask
[Fig. 4.3.3]**
In two of the three cases, the system is able to identify both the markers and the movable object exclusively, without any difficulty. In the third image (Fig 4.3.3), part of a shadow formed by one of the non-critical objects is of a colour and intensity (black), and of a significant enough size, to be wrongly identified as part of a marker. This is a problem specific to greyscale (rather than colour) markers, and again highlights why colour marker colours are often used (Note that the object mask, of a vivid red colour, was successfully and exclusively identified in all three cases). Even this erroneously included portion of the feature could be successfully identified as erroneous by the computer and removed by, for instance, further erosion filtering, or simply (as, in the prototype case, there are just four markers) retaining only the largest object in each quarter of the mask. 
Where erroneously identified objects exist that are of similar or greater size to the correctly identified mask features, a more complicated system - that, for example, determines which of the feature objects identified in the mask is most likely to be the real one based on the known marker geometry – would have to be used.
Effects Of Lighting
So far, all examples shown have been ones where ambient lighting has been of a normal level; the image was neither dark nor light, and the camera neither over exposed or underexposed. The following images show some of the effects of different types of ambient lighting conditions, including many which are sub-optimal:

Table of lighting effects on object identification
[Table. 4.3.1]**
As stated previously, using a greyscale marker represents a worst-case scenario regarding ease of identification. This shows in these results, where the shadow formed by one particular non-critical object, near the top right of the bounded area, is erroneously identified in almost every marker mark of the image with sub-optimal lighting. In images 2 and 3, even more objects are included erroneously. In no cases, however, are the correct objects not identified, and only in image 3 does an erroneous feature ‘bleed’ into a marker (making removing erroneous features exclusively more difficult). 
In comparison, the red object was correctly and exclusively identified in almost all cases. When the camera was greatly overexposed (Image 4), the program was unable to differentiate between the object and its background, and thus it was not identified. In the other case (Image 5), part of the edge of the image was overexposed, and incorrectly identified. However, as the object is known to not be on the edge of the image, and the erroneous feature was not connected to it, the erroneous feature could identified as such and removed quite easily.
The results of image 4 are important, as, in this case, the markers were easily identified. This type of image could be created by either increasing the contrast of the camera, or by switching on a portable light source behind the camera. This would have the effect of removing the effect of uneven lighting on creating erroneous feature identification (such as in image 5), and more easily than attempting to block light. Afterwards, the light source could be de-activated to allow the red object to be identified.
In a more general case, a strong light source that makes features of a certain colour more prominent could be used to make identifying that particular feature easier.
Effects Of Camera Distance
So far, all tests have been conducted at a single distance (154mm). The following table shows the effect changing the distance has on the calculated ratio of right side vertical distance between markers to left side distance- used to calculate the horizontal plane angle of the camera to the object plane – at 30 degrees to the left of the normal angle.

Masks used to calculate ratios at same angle and different distances
[Fig. 4.3.10]**
As one can see, as the distance increases, the ratio tends towards 1. This makes sense due to the effects a limited resolution has on the way an object is interpreted at different distances. As the object gets further away, more information about it is lost, and less can be interpreted about it. Even looking at the masks of the images, they start to appear more rectangular when smaller, and this effect is resulting in the increasingly inaccurate ratio results.
It would be possible to use the actual number of pixels between markers, and the size of markers in the images, to estimate the distance from the camera to the plane, and thus the accuracy of other figures such as estimated angle. 
In DIC, is important that the two images to be correlated are at the same distance (as well as angle). Thus, a final system would need to be able to determine and inform the user of its distance from the plane, as well as angle to it.  
4.4 Calibration
For stage 1b, it was required that the prototype device be able to determine an angle from a ratio. This required prior calibration: 10 sets of 17 images, at a set distance and horizontal plane angles varying incrementally from –40 degrees to 40 degrees in 5 degree steps, were taken, and the ratio between left vertical edge and right vertical edge (as in the previous example) were found. 
The average value at each of these angles, from all 10 cases, was found, giving the following results when plotted against angle:
Graph of ratio to angle
[Fig. 4.4.1]
A third degree polynomial approximation, equating ratio to angle, was found, and the coefficients generated stored. The Matlab functions polyfit and polytool were used, and the red lines on the above graph show the polytool estimated error level of the polynomial fit to the real values. These are loaded into the stage 1b code, and used to calculate the horizontal plane angle.
The coefficients, in descending order, are: 272.6;   -914.3; 1,152.0;   -509.9

title: Chapter 8: Invalidity Benefit and Incapacity Benefit
text:
Source: Pages 135–162 of MintonThesis.pdf (September 2009). Text extracted from PDF; figures extracted directly as images.

8 Invalidity Benefit and Incapacity Benefit 8.1 Introduction The purpose of this chapter is to learn more about how the labour market has changed over the last thirty years, in order to provide an appropriate historical and social context from which the „five year topic‟ covered in the next three chapters, the piloting and expansion of a programme of welfare reform known as Pathways to Work, can be better understood. As with the previous chapter, I will take broadly orthogonal swipes through the phenomena, first using longitudinal statistical data, to identify salient trends in the labour market and claimant rates over the last three chapters; then using relatively recent cross-sectional data to identify prominent demographic features in the IB claimant population, and the working age economically inactive more generally.
8.2 On the Conditions Under Which the Qualities of Government Benefit (And Related) Statistics Arose Collecting and storing data on people is usually expensive, both in terms of time and money, and so it seems sensible to assume that there are reasons why such data are stored. Broadly, and not withstanding various nuances and complexities, it seems sensible to assume that the data institutions collect are related to the functions they perform. To understand more about government statistics, therefore, it helps to have at least a rudimentary understanding of what the functions of the British Welfare State are, and of what they used to be. What follows is thus an attempt to present a very partial discussion of the original intentions underlying the British Welfare State, some developments to the system since, and the main details of systems of provision at the time of writing, with respect to social security systems for those of working age and long-term sick. These represent my interpretation of what I assume to be a privileged account of the development of Invalidity and Incapacity Benefit over the course of the twentieth century. The account is privileged because it is found in the introduction to a book intended for practicing lawyers, specialising in the field of British social security legislation, and so, where there is ambiguity in the interpretation and application of the legislation, this account may be relied upon to guide those practicing law in this field towards the „correct‟ interpretation. Pages 2-7 of the Fourth Edition of Ogus & Wikeley‟s book The Law of
 Social Security164 provide this privileged account, my interpretation of which is as follows: 1. A tapestry of local social provisioning systems had already emerged during and before the twentieth century, to provide for the needs of members in times of greater hardship. Meanwhile, the general level of state power increases, and a number of these local provisioning systems find themselves complemented with, superseded by, regulated by, or consolidated into a set of national systems. However, this nationalisation of provisioning systems occurred in a „haphazard‟ manner, with some social services being provided increasingly by the state; and other, complementary and overlapping systems, being provided much more extensively by local organisations. 2. The increasingly shared experiences of risk and common fate which occurred during the Second World War meant the national government was ceded additional central control by various local socioeconomic interests. This provided an opportunity for the haphazard system of social security the government was in charge of to be „rationalised‟, systematised, and turned into a comprehensive system that ensures a minimum, sufficient, standard of existence for all British systems. Ironically, the model for this system was one of social insurance, and inspired by that observed in the nation with whom Britain was presently at war. 3. The shared fate and sense of national identity of British citizens waned, and so the degree to which the average citizen would allow the government further extensive involvement in their lives waned. Instead, future adjustments to the Welfare State were „variations on a theme‟. During the 1970s and before, attempts were made to update and adjust the Beveridge model, while remaining ideologically „friendly‟ to it. 4. During the 1980s, adjustments were made to the Welfare State which were based on ideological hostility to many of the principles and practices of the Beveridge model, because of perceptions of the deleterious consequences „welfare dependency‟, corrosion of traditional family values and the strength of the community, and discouragement from thrift – of a welfare system that was seen as „too comprehensive‟. However, the ironic consequence of
164
Ogus, A. I. and N. J. Wikeley (1995). The Law of Social Security. London, Butterworths.
 Thatcherite economic policies was to induce mass unemployment, and thus make far more people dependent upon state aid. Concurrent with the rise in unemployment during this last period, was a rise in working age long-term sickness benefit claimants. This led to a further degree of dependence, for millions of people, upon state welfare, and thus – from the government‟s accounting perspective – another unanticipated and significant form of expenditure. Most of the longitudinal statistics to be presented here cover a period of time from the last part of stage three, to the present time. Depending upon one‟s interpretation of events, the present time could either be seen as a continuation of stage four („LateThatcherite‟); or as a new stage („Post-Thatcherite‟). Either way, I will show that the policies, possibilities, and ideologies of each of these eras have had a significant influence upon present-day circumstances, which will themselves be considered in more detail.
8.3 Longitudinal Investigations In 2007, the Department of Work and Pensions commissioned David Freud, a former investment banker, to write a report providing advice on how to reduce benefit claimant numbers.165 The Freud Report presents a graph summarising some of the shorter-term (1993 onwards) longitudinal patterns in reason for inactivity. This is reproduced as






Figure 1: ‘Reasons for Inactivity’



Figure 8.1 below.
165
Freud, D. (2007). “Reducing dependency, increasing opportunity: options for the future of welfare to work.” Retrieved 26 June, 2009, from http://www.dwp.gov.uk/publications/dwp/2007/welfarereview.pdf.
 Figure 8.1 ‘Reasons for Inactivity’ Source & title from Fig. 5 of Freud, D. (2007) ‘Reducing dependency, increasing opportunity: options for the future of welfare to work’, London: CDS
Some observations are worth noting: The rank order of each of the economically inactive categories has remained the same throughout the time series. „Looking after the family or home‟ has always accounted for more „inactivity‟ than has being long-term sick, which has in turn accounted for more „inactivity‟ than being a student, and so on. No qualitative transitions of this kind have occurred during the time period. However, the three main reasons – looking after the home, long-term sickness, and being a student – for economic inactivity have increasingly converged in magnitude, towards a figure of about two million claimants each. Working age (i.e. „early‟) retirement has been an increasing cause of economic inactivity. The official numbers of „discouraged workers‟ has always been a relatively small number, but has reduced further to a small fraction of its original magnitude. Within the time period 1993-2006, then, the relative composition, and size, of the working age economically inactive population has remained relatively stable. Broader changes in benefit receipts – including IB and IVB, have been more dramatic, however,
 over the time period 1979-2005, as indicated in






Figure 2: Number of people on benefits, 1979-2005



Figure 8.2, also taken from The Freud Report.
Figure 8.2 Number of people on benefits, 1979-2005 Source: ONS, Department for Work and Pensions Information Directorate, Work and Pensions Longitudinal Study
Although the graph is technically mislabelled - Incapacity Benefit did not exist prior to 1995, and so working-age long-term sick people prior to this period could not have claimed it (instead they claimed Invalidity Benefit) – it highlights a very significant transition in the composition of the working age population: During the late 1970s and 1980s, long-term sickness (IVB) claimants were fewer in number than were unemployment benefit claimants. The levels rose, however, at some point during the 1990s, long-term sickness (IB) claimant rates came to exceed unemployment benefit claimant rates. 8.3.1 Sickness and Unemployment Benefits: Some comparisons The previous paragraph, however, describes IVB/IB claimant levels relative to a „moving target‟: that of unemployment benefit receipts. Unemployment benefit rates have, looking again at Figure 8.2, been above three million (in 1986), and below one million (from 2001 onwards). Unemployment levels – and changes in unemployment levels - during this period of observation have been much more variable than those of long-term sickness benefit receipts.
 One, fairly intuitive, way of quantifying this difference in variability between IVB/IB benefit receipts, and unemployment rates, is to compare the sum of the modulus (magnitude) of the differences from consecutive years between the two benefit types, over the same period of observations.166 For the years 1975-2007 inclusive, this produces values of 6499 for ILO unemployment, and 2273 for IVB/IB;167 the ratio between these two values is 2.86, so by this measure ILO unemployment levels have been almost three times as variable as IVB/IB levels during this time period. 8.3.2 Exploring Longitudinal Patterns in More Detail The data used to calculate the above metric, taken from a variety of sources, are plotted in






Figure 3: IVB/IB and ILO Unemployment levels within UK, 1975-2007



Figure 8.3.168 Although this graph is broadly the same as that displayed in the Freud Report, it differs in at least two subtle, but perhaps important, respects.
Figure 8.3 IVB/IB and ILO Unemployment levels within UK, 1975-2007 Sources: Social Security Statistics (courtesy of Duncan McVicar); the Labour Force Survey; claimant count data; Accessed 29 February 2008, from www.nomis.co.uk; and http://www.statistics.gov.uk/StatBase/tsdataset.asp?vlnk=429&Pos=2&ColRank=1&Rank=272
166
i.e. where is the value of the observation (either IVB/IB, or ILO Unemployment) at time t, and y is observed for t 167 The data sources used in this calculation are described in the caption of Figure 8.3. 168 Unlike the graph used in The Freud Report, which are based on survey data, the values presented in this graph are based on actual claimant count data – and hence are a ‘population’ rather than a ‘sample’.
 Firstly, whereas the Freud Report graph suggested that IVB/IB graphs represent a fairly smooth and gradual transition from a fairly low to a fairly high number of claimants over the time period (like a sigmoid or logistic regression curve), these figures instead suggest changes in IVB/IB rates instead have a more „hinge-like‟ shape, and look more like three straight lines: 1. Line one, from 1975 to 1987: very slightly positive but almost flat. Beginning at 934,000 claimants, and ending at 1,155,000 claimants. 2. Line two, from 1987 to 1995: steep upwards climb. Beginning at 1,155,000 claimants, and rising to 2,406,000 claimants 3. Line three, from 1995 to present: relatively flat but more variable than previous two lines. Starting at 2,406,000 claimants, ending at 2,414,000 claimants, and varying in the intermediate period between 2,260,000 claimants (in 2000) and 2500,000 claimants (in 2004) The important change in the IVB/IB rates, therefore, occurred during the period 19871995 („line two‟). It may not be a co-incidence that 1987 marked the start of the steepest drop in ILO unemployed (from 3.1 million to 2.5 million in 1988, followed by 2.1 million in 1989) for over a decade; and that 1995 was the year in which Invalidity Benefit (IVB) was replaced by Incapacity Benefit (IB), which is generally accepted as having much tougher entry criteria. A second notable feature of the claimant count graph is the unusually steep rise in ILO unemployment from the period 2005-2006, from 1.43 million, to 1.66 million (and rising to 1.67 million in 2007). Though this may simply be a particularly large instantiation of the natural, year-on-year, variability in unemployment levels („noise‟), it might also represent early signs of a new „shift in the fundamentals‟ („signal‟). 8.3.3 Longitudinal Comparisons between IVB/IB and other Longitudinal Statistics A great many things have, of course, changed since 1975 in addition to the level of working age long-term sickness benefit claimants. In this section, we will observe, both graphically and statistically, the co-development of a number of statistical and demographic factors concurrently with incapacity benefit claims, in order to try to understand how such factors may be related to one another.
 8.3.4 IVB/IB and Working Age Population Firstly, one relationship worth noting is that the IVB/IB claimant levels over the period 1975-2007 have been strongly correlated (r= 0.87) with changes in the number of people of working age over that same period, as shown in






Figure 4: Working age population and IVB/IB claimant numbers, 1975-2007



Figure 8.4.
Figure 8.4 Working age population and IVB/IB claimant numbers, 1975-2007 Sources: See Figure 8.3
However, as mentioned previously, the rise in IVB/IB numbers appears to be a story in three parts, with the majority of the increase during the observation period occurring during just eight years: from 1987 to 1995. The size of the working age population, however, has grown throughout the period, and so it would seem naïve to assume that the rise in claimant count is „just‟ a demographic one.






Figure 5: IVB/IB claimants per 1000 people of working age, 1975-2007



Figure 8.5 „controls for‟ this broad demographic factor by plotting total claimant rates over this period (more specifically, the number of IVB/IB claimants per 1000 people of working age):
 Figure 8.5 IVB/IB claimants per 1000 people of working age, 1975-2007 Sources: See Figure 8.3
If anything, this „correction‟ shown in makes the three part structure of the claimant count rise even more prominent, with the disjuncture between the three periods (19751987; 1987-1995; 1995-2007) even more apparent. Claimant count rates more than doubled over the central period, but not before or since. 8.3.5 IVB/IB and Female Working Age Economic Activity An intuitive, if sometimes reticently admitted, assumption about the reason for increases in IVB/IB claimant levels, amongst men at least, is that there are now more women in the labour market, making it more competitive and thus harder for those less „fit‟ (whether physically, psychologically, or „intellectually‟) to remain within it. To some extent, there seems to be some reality in this assumption, although the likely paths of causal influence are hard to disentangle. To begin with,






Figure 6: Numbers of male IVB/IB claimants and female working age economic activity rates



Figure 8.6 plots the changes in the proportion of the female working age population who are „economically active‟ (either in or seeking employment) alongside the male IB/IVB claim rate:
 Figure 8.6 Numbers of male IVB/IB claimants and female working age economic activity rates Sources: See Figure 8.3
The two series exhibit an apparently strong correlation (r= 0.83). However, females do not simply seem to have „displaced‟ males. Rather, as females have become more economically active, and engaged in the labour market, so they have found themselves facing increasingly similar labour market pressures, including those which lead people onto IVB/IB. One way of seeing this is to plot the number of female IVB/IB claimants, per 1000 male claimants, over this time period, as shown in






Figure 7: Female IVB/IB claimants per 1000 male IVB/IB claimants, 1975-2007



Figure 8.7.
 Figure 8.7 Female IVB/IB claimants per 1000 male IVB/IB claimants, 1975-2007 Sources: See Figure 8.3
This shows that female and male claimant rates have been moving closer to parity, almost linearly,169 for the last thirty-two years. Another comparison which may be of interest is to compare changes, since 1975, in the ratio of IVB/IB populations to overall working age population, for males, females, and both males and females. This is shown in






Figure 8: Index of IVB/IB population to overall working population



Figure 8.8, where 1975 values are indexed at 100:
169
A linear regression of the values estimates parity in 2042, converging at a rate of 14.8 additional females per 1000 males per year.
 Figure 8.8 Index of IVB/IB population to overall working population, for males, females, and combined (1975=100) Sources: See Figure 8.3
This shows that, during the period 1987-95, the female claimant index grew with a much steeper gradient than the male index. Additionally, whereas, after 1995, the male claimant index began to decline slightly, the female index continued to rise. 8.3.6 Cohort Effects and Social Class The preceding discussions have attempted to disentangle demographic factors from other factors. We have seen that, although IVB/IB claimant levels have risen alongside population size, they have done so at a much faster rate, and with a „hinge-shaped‟ and somewhat disjoint pattern of growth, rather than the kind of smooth, curvilinear shape one might expect if demographic factors were the primary cause (and as suggested in Figure 8.2, reproduced from The Freud Report). One way of trying to disentangle other, socioeconomic, factors from demographic factors is to look at cohorts: the differences and similarities between peoples at the same stages in their life-courses – the same age – but born at a different times.






Figure 9: Inactivity rates for men with high qualifications by age group and birth cohort



Figure 8.9 presents the economic inactivity levels for five different cohort groups, at ages ranging from 25 to 64, with at least an A-level:
 Figure 8.9 ‘Inactivity rates for men with high levels of qualifications [A levels and above] by age group and birth cohort; 1986 to 2001’ (Original image in colour) Source: Figure 4b of Barham, C. (2002) “Patterns of economic inactivity among older men”, Labour Market Trends, June 2002, pp. 301-310 (Original Data Source: Labour Force Survey)
For better-qualified males in society, economic inactivity is largely a demographic factor: each cohort experiences broadly the same likelihood of inactivity when it reaches the same age, with a sharp increase in economic inactivity during the final five-to-ten years of the working age timespan. For those with no qualifications, however, the story is very different, as shown in Figure 8.10.
 






Figure 10: Inactivity rates for men with no qualifications by age group and birth cohort



Figure 8.10 ‘Inactivity rates for men with no qualifications by age group and birth cohort; United Kingdom; 1986 to 2001’ (Original image in colour) Source: Figure 4a of Barham, C. (2002) “Patterns of economic inactivity among older men”, Labour Market Trends, June 2002, pp. 301-310 (Original Data Source: Labour Force Survey)
8.4 On the relationship between sickness, employment, and social class amongst males 8.4.1 Introduction and Summary There has been a dramatic change in the relationship between sickness, employment, and social class, but this drama has played out slowly and quietly over the last three decades, and so occurred largely unnoticed. In 1996, however, a five-page British Medical Journal paper by Mel Bartley and Charlie Owen,170 presented some results which showed a dramatic change in relationship between social class, economic inactivity, sickness, and unemployment. The graphs presented in that paper plot the changing labour markets of male workers, of various social classes, and levels of health. They show that, over time, employment has 170
Bartley, M. and C. Owen (1996). “Relation between socioeconomic status, employment, and health during economic change, 1973-93.” British Medical Journal 313(7055): 445-449.
 become increasingly a function of social class, with those people whose work is more physical, manual, in nature less likely to be in employment. They investigate this further, by sub-dividing each class into those who have self-identified as having a „limiting longstanding illness‟, and those who have not. For each class, those who answered in the affirmative were less likely to be employed, across all time periods. This disparity, on average, grew from 1973 to 1993. It grew much faster, however, for the „lower‟ classes, whose work was of a more manual and (from the perspective of formal qualifications) „unskilled‟ nature. Disadvantages for manual and formally unskilled workers had thus been compounded substantially over the last twenty years: the job security of such types of work has decreased; the level of pay –relative to non-manual jobs – has decreased; and the detrimental effect of sickness and injury on one‟s employability has increased. The article also shows that this labour market disadvantage of manual labourers has not manifested itself very substantially –though it has manifested itself moderately substantially - in unemployment figures. Though, for each class, the level of recorded unemployment for those with limiting longstanding illness (henceforth „LLTI‟) was slightly higher than for those without, this gap was not large, and even crossed over slightly for some classes and for some years. For all classes, the unemployment levels of those with and without LLTIs followed each other closely, showing up-turns and down-turns characteristic of changing general economic circumstances. The article then answers a conundrum suggested by the previous three figures: if employment has gone down, but unemployment has not gone up by the same amount, then what has happened? It shows that the reduction in employment – with those with LLTIs suffering the blunt of the employment effect - has been complemented much more closely by a rise in working age economic inactivity than working age unemployment. As I will show, this relationship can be statistically quantified through correlation coefficients, as well as appearing the most visually intuitive interpretation of the evidence. 8.4.2 Replication The British Medical Journal summarised above was published in 1996, and uses data from the General Household Survey up until 1993. I wished to find out whether the patterns shown over the twenty-year period 1973 to 1993 had been maintained or altered in years since. To do this, I attempted to use the datasets (the General Household
 Survey) and follow the methods described in the original article as closely as possible, in order, firstly, to replicate the original findings; and secondly, to update the results to include the effects of – for example – a new, centre-Left, government regime. The results of the attempt at replicating the results from 1973 to 1993, and updating the results to cover the years up until 2003, are shown here as figures 8.11, 8.12, 8.13, and 8.14 below. Although some of the particular percentages are not exactly the same – perhaps due to differences in the coding schemes adopted – all of the major qualitative patterns observed in the original research have been replicated. The changes and continuities observed in the period 1994-2003, i.e. in the new part of the research, deserve noting.






Figure 11: Proportions employed according to socioeconomic group, 1975-2003



Figure 8.11 Proportions employed according to socioeconomic group, 1975-2003 Source: General Household Survey, based on Figure 1 of Bartley, M. And Owen, C (1996) ‘Relation between socioeconomic status, employment, and health during economic change, 1973-93’, BMJ 13(7055): 445-449
 If one first considers Figure 8.11, based upon Figure 1 of the original research, one sees that 1993, the final year of the original analysis, turned out to be a point of inflection, rather than a mid-point in a trend, with employment rates rising in the years since, for all classes. The general level of working age employment, however, is lower for all classes than in 1973. The employment levels of the „top‟ three social classes – professional/managerial, intermediate, and skilled manual – have begun to converge, producing a wide gap between these classes and the semi/unskilled manual class, which was characteristic of the data even in 1973.






Figure 12: Proportion employed according to limiting longstanding illness and socioeconomic group



Figure 8.12 Proportion employed according to limiting longstanding illness and socioeconomic group Dashed lines indicate no LLTI; full lines indicate LLTI. Classes: top left: ‘Professional/managerial’; top right: ‘Intermediate non-manual’; bottom left: ‘Skilled manual’; bottom right: ‘Semi and unskilled’ Source: General Household Survey. Based on Figure 2 of Bartley, M. And Owen, C (1996) ‘Relation between socioeconomic status, employment, and health during economic change, 1973-93’, BMJ 13(7055): 445-449
 Figure 8.12, which updates Figure 2 of the original research, shows some further evidence of a somewhat improved general economy, but with differential effects for those of different classes and health statuses. For each class, the differentials between those with and those without LLTIs seem roughly to observe the following pattern: during labour market downturns, the employment rate goes down, but faster for those with LLTIs than those without. Conversely, during labour market upturns, the employment rate goes up, but faster for those without LLTIs than those with. Both when the labour market is good, and when it is bad, the effect on the differential fortunes of those with and without LLTIs is the same: a widening in the employment gap between people, of all classes, on the grounds of health, which is especially pronounced amongst those of poorer health.






Figure 13: Proportions unemployed according to limiting longstanding illness and socioeconomic group



Figure 8.13 Proportions unemployed according to limiting longstanding illness and socioeconomic group Dashed lines indicate no LLTI; full lines indicate LLTI. Classes: top left: ‘Professional/managerial’; top right: ‘Intermediate non-manual’; bottom left: ‘Skilled manual’; bottom right: ‘Semi and unskilled’ Source: General Household Survey. Based on Figure 3 of Bartley, M. And Owen, C (1996) ‘Relation between socioeconomic status, employment, and health during economic change, 1973-93’, BMJ 13(7055): 445-449
 Figure 8.13, based on Figure 3 of the original research, continues the broad pattern observed in Figure 8.12: differentials between unemployment amongst those with and without LLTIs are small, and if anything have decreased slightly over the period 19942003.






Figure 14: Proportions inactive according to limiting longstanding illness and socioeconomic group



Figure 8.14 Proportions inactive according to limiting longstanding illness and socioeconomic group Dashed lines indicate no LLTI; full lines indicate LLTI. Classes: top left: ‘Professional/managerial’; top right: ‘Intermediate non-manual’; bottom left: ‘Skilled manual’; bottom right: ‘Semi and unskilled’ Source: General Household Survey. Based on Figure 4 of Bartley, M. And Owen, C (1996) ‘Relation between socioeconomic status, employment, and health during economic change, 1973-93’, BMJ 13(7055): 445-449
Figure 8.14, though it does not reproduce quite the same magnitude of the effects shown in Figure 4 of the original research on which it was based (again probably due to different coding/operationalisation of the problem), shows the same qualitative patterns, and shows them continuing and, for most classes, expanding over the additional decade. For the „lowest‟ three classes, the level of inactivity amongst those with LLTIs has
 increased, almost linearly as a function of time.171 For these three classes, the magnitude of the gradient increases as the social class „decreases‟. 8.4.3 Correlations Another way of understanding the data is to observe the correlations between percentages of the male working age population unemployed, employed, and economically inactive, for both of the two possible health conditions (has LLTI/does not have LLTI); and all four of the possible social classes; for each comparable year in the GHS between 1973 and 2003 inclusive. This produces a four-by-two cell table, each cell containing the three possible correlations of the three variables. The results of doing this are shown in Table 8.1, and lead to some interesting observations:
171
In fact, if one were to run four simple linear regressions of inactivity rates regressed against year, one for each social class, the estimated gradients produced (i.e. the ß1 coefficient values) are as follows: Professional/managerial class: 0.39 % rate increase in inactivity per year Intermediate (non-manual) class: 0.42% increase per year Skilled manual class: 0.72% increase per year Semi-skilled and unskilled manual class: 0.99 % increase per year
 unemployment unemployment
employment

-0.33 unemployment -0.82 -0.27 inactivity
unemployment
unemployment inactivity

Semi and Unskilled Manual

-0.96 -0.49 0.24
-0.99 -0.34 0.17 unemployment
unemployment inactivity
