This interpretation of codes, formalised written documents operating as „symbolic authority figures‟, and their functions within bureaucratic rule, suggests that, in order to understand how political power is exercised in the modern age, attention should be paid both to the details, and to the historical conditions of formation, of the technical and administrative documents defining procedures, categories, and rules. When investigating processes of political-bureaucratic continuity and change, however, such documents only tell part of the story. To use Tilly‟s terminology again, these documents offer formulas, which Tilly suggests “identify an appropriate correspondence between Y (the event, action or outcome at hand) and X (its antecedent), but enter little or not at all
Tilly, C. (2006). Why? what happens when people give reasons … and why. Oxford, Princeton University Press., p. 103, emphasis added
 into the causal chain connecting Y to X.”101 They do not offer cause-effect accounts, which “trace causal lines from X to Y”.102 Tilly divides both formulas and cause-effect accounts into „popular‟ and „specialised‟ variants, as shown in Table 5.1.
Formulas Cause-Effect Accounts
Popular Conventions Stories
Specialized Codes Technical Accounts
Table 5.1 Charles Tilly’s 4 types of reasons Source: Redrawn from page 19 of Tilly, C. (2006), Why? What happens when people give reasons… and why, Princeton: Princeton University Press
Tilly refers to „popular‟ (as in, not requiring extensive specialised training to articulate or understand) cause-effect accounts as stories. Cause-effect accounts, Tilly argues, tend to be offered to people of similar social rank, whereas formulas are given to those of lower rank. In order to understand why particular codes are implemented within a bureaucratic structure, therefore, one also needs to know something about the types of stories, causal „models of the world‟, popular amongst those in command of the structure. Although formulas do not offer reasons why one should do Y in the event of X, one can assume that, often, they are formulated and prescribed because their application is assumed to cause a desired outcome; driving many codes, therefore, are stories.
5.7 The Self-sustaining Qualities of Bureaucratic Structures In a complex biological organism, discussed in chapter 2, there are broadly two types of way in which its brain and central nervous system can influence the behaviour of many of its peripheral aspects: either it can sent a very fast, directed, electrical signal to a given peripheral component (like a finger), in which case the central system comes to yield a specific response upon the peripheral system, very quickly, but for only a brief duration (the finger commanded by an electrical signal to contract towards the palm will not remain in that contracted state for hours, days or months after the command has been issued); or, it can (initially via electrical signals) cause changes in the chemical composition, the milieu, surrounding the peripheral system, leading to a slower, less directed, but more durable influence. For many cells and systems of cells, repeated exposure to particular patterns of electrical or chemical signal leads to long-term changes to their autonomous mode of operation. 101
Ibid., p. 20 Ibid., p. 20. Tilly continues: “even if we observers find those causal lines absurd or incomprehensible.” 102
 To what extent is it wise to pursue the analogy between biological organisms and bureaucratic organisations? Furthermore, if one were to pursue the analogy, what sort of organism might a bureaucracy best be compared to? An amoeba? An insect? A small mammal?
What possible isomorphisms, identifiable commonalities, exist between
organisms and organisations? Some suggestions are as follows: Formal rules, which accrete and emerge over time in response to new general classes of situation, or are emulated and adapted from other organisations, produce complex, specific, stereotyped patterns of response to a given, identified situation, and thus bear some qualitative similarity to the emotions and dispositional tendencies of organisms. Fast communication technology, such as email or telephone, allows the quick relaying of signals to or from a site of central command; the central authority can then make a quick, specific decisions with respect to a very local aspect of the organisation – for example telling the head of a particular department that their request for funding for a particular project has been granted or rejected; or the central authority may choose to make more general changes to the organisation: asking the legal or quality-control department to draft new codes of practice by which employees within the organisation as a whole should thereafter conform (analogous inducing an emotion or creating a new dispositional memory). So long as one is aware of the extent to which the above represents analogy rather than exact homology of function, I suggest that comparisons between organisms and organisations are useful in indicating why, in bureaucracies, paperwork is such an integral feature of their general coherence and function. At the very least, and in a very general, cybernetics or systems theory sense, it should certainly not be controversial to state that bureaucratic organisations, as well as biological organisations, appear to exhibit the qualities of being: 1. Bounded: with a clear, categorical demarcation between within and without. Boundaries are almost always clearly identified, such that individuals working as functionaries within bureaucratic structures tend to be both able and willing to identify the name of the organisation within which they are working.
 2. Self-regulating: in that perturbations occurring at the local level (to a particular aspect of the system) are generally mitigated such that they do not tend to lead to the collapse of the global coherence of the system. (For example, if a departmental lawyer chooses to quit the company and move elsewhere, there are procedures in place – for example, a substantial period of notice and procedures for internal and external recruitment - to ensure that the role of departmental lawyer „survives‟ the loss of the individual.) 3. Self-repairing: In the sense that a global coherence of the organisation is usually maintained even as and when local aspects of the structure are replaced. The maintenance of global coherence in the face of absolute local change is best illustrated through the example of the Ship of Theseus: a ship that has existed so long, and been subject to so many repairs, that no part of it is original structure remains; the same logic underlies a particular pattern of joke: “This is my grandfather‟s axe: the handle has been changed three times and the head four times but it‟s still the same axe”. Unlike in these examples, however, the prefix self- suggests that the repair occurs due to internal rather than external volitions and actions. Together, these three qualities help explain much of the self-sustaining „nature‟ of bureaucratic structures. As Weber noted, the structures often even survive the loss of their most senior nodes: During the course of his long years in power, Bismarck brought his ministerial colleagues into unconditional bureaucratic dependence by eliminating all independent statesmen. Upon his retirement, he saw to his surprise that they continued to manage their offices unconcerned and undismayed, as if he had not been the master mind and creator of these creatures, but rather as if some single figure had been exchanged for some other figure in the bureaucratic machine.103 This above quote also suggests something else important to note about bureaucratic rule: As with the emergence of complex and unequal nation-states (Chapter 3), the bureaucratic structure, by eliminating prior social structures, is autocatalytic, causing increasing demand for and dependence upon itself. To consider the issue in ecological terms, one might say that the bureaucratic structure, like a beaver building a dam or a spider building a web, is able to adapt aspects of its environment to make it more suited 103
Weber, M. (1946). The Bureaucratic Machine. Social Theory: The multicultural and classic readings. C. Lemert. Oxford, Westview Press: 104-110., p. 110
 to its own requirements. In the case of bureaucracies, we are its environment, and we have, in various ways, been adapted to become more consonant with bureaucratic living.
crumbs: Chapter 5: Bureaucracies and Organisations: Some Theories of Large Scale Social Phenomena

title: Chapter 2: Organisms, Decisions and Emotions: Some Theories of Very Small Scale Social Phenomena
text:
Source: Pages 23–39 of MintonThesis.pdf (September 2009). Text extracted from PDF; figures extracted directly as images.

2 Organisms, Decisions and Emotions: Some Theories of Very Small Scale Social Phenomena 2.1 Introduction The topics covered within this chapter are perhaps the least intuitive of those covered within the entire thesis, and as such this chapter may be more challenging than many of those that follow. Paradoxically, this is because the contents of this chapter include the topic of intuition itself: what it is and how it emerged. This is because having intuitions – as well as feelings, suspicions, beliefs, thoughts, and habits – is part of what it means to be members of a particular type of complex organism (human beings), and this chapter is about organisms, both in the more specific sense of the human organism, and in a more general sense. It considers the ontology, the essential features and qualities, of „the organism‟ as a particular way of thinking about structures and relationships between elements. The purposes of this chapter include: 1. To highlight the extent to which conscious acts such as thinking, seeing, imagining, and deciding are related to, and dependent upon, unconscious acts such as breathing, sensing, feeling, and responding. 2. To suggest the mutually interdependent relationship between „higher‟ mental functions (those of which we are conscious) and „lower‟ mental functions (those of which we are not conscious), and thus to make the case that, in order to understand the operation of „higher‟ functions, „lower‟ functions must also be understood. 3. Following from the previous point, to illustrate the importance of emotional responses in decision-making; and conversely, to present the theory that aspects of the brain and body function as emotional „simulators‟ (or emotional-stimulus replicators). 4. To suggest some adaptive benefits, and thus the selective forces, that may lead to the development of „higher‟ mental functions. 5. To illustrate the extent to which the majority of individuals‟ behaviours, actions, and performances are automated and unconscious, rather than consciously evaluated and pre-selected; as a corollary, to indicate the extent to which consciousness – and choice-making (a conscious act) – may be thought of, from the perspective of the organism, as a „burden‟ to be avoided where possible, rather than as something to be „sought‟ or „maximised‟.
 6. To present a conceptual distinction, and description of the relationship, between the „organism‟ and the „environment‟; and between the „subject‟ and the „object‟. In order to avoid having to do the additional work of developing a conceptual and terminological lexicon, as well as learning a new set of theories, this discussion will be anchored around the theories and descriptions provided by a single theorist, the neuroscientist Antonio Damasio, and on accounts presented within his book, The Feeling of What Happens4. By detailing the essential qualities of organisms, I will present not just a relatively novel description of social beings, but also a set of concepts and associated terminology, derived from analysis of one kind of emergent phenomena, which may be of use in later chapters when attempting to understand other phenomena. In particular, within later chapters I will attempt to identify similarities between the functioning of (very-small scale) biological organisms and (large scale) bureaucratic organisations. Illustrative questions include: To what extent do legal documents perform similar tasks within organisations as emotions do within organisms? To what extent do organisational roles and management hierarchies have similar information-processing qualities as those observed within organisms? To what extent do both organisms and organisations „learn‟, and become „habituated‟ to respond to particular forms of (external) environmental stimuli in the same ways? To what extent are organisations like organisms in their quest for self-replication and self-preservation?
2.2 Health Inequalities and Human Physiology To begin with, consider the following description of the „psychosocial model‟ of health inequalities: The original ideas behind [the psychosocial model] were based on the existence of two types of response to external threats shared by both humans and other animals. … The most well known … is the so called „fight or flight‟ response.
4
Damasio, A. R. (2000). The feeling of what happens : body and emotion in the making of consciousness. London, W. Heinemann.
 The body received alarm messages from the brain that can be thought of as activating one or other of two „response circuits‟ […] [The sympathetic-adrenomedullary circuit] involves the sympathetic nervous system, which, in balance with the parasympathetic nervous system, is responsible for much of what goes on in a routine way in the body without conscious awareness. They govern how fast the heart is beating, response to changes in outside temperature (shivering, sweating) and the pH (acidity) level of the blood, for example. Sympathetic nervous stimulation releases adrenaline from the adrenal medulla, and at the same time the ends of the sympathetic nerves themselves release noradrenaline. This prompts the release into the bloodstream of fibrinogen, a substance that helps blood to clot more easily. At the same time, the heart rate and blood pressure rise, and the small blood vessels (capillaries) nearest to the skin narrow sharply. This would prevent excessive blood loss from a wound, but it also has the effect of raising the blood pressure.5 To use terms and concepts which will be described in more detail later in the chapter, one could say the following: the release of fibrinogen, the increase in heart rate and blood pressure, the narrowing of capillaries – are all automatic, unconscious components of what Damasio calls “complex, stereotyped patterns of response”6, known collectively as an „emotion‟ – and are the chronic, proximate causes of heart diseases which exhibit a well observed and consistent social gradient. One can treat such proximate causes – for example, through the consumption of aspirin, which thins the blood – in order to counter the „abnormal‟, physiological imbalance of the human organism. However, following the logic of the psychosocial hypothesis, this „abnormality‟, within the blood of the organism, results directly from a normal response to an abnormal environment. From the perspective to be developed within this chapter, the above can be understood by thinking about the relationship between a biological organism and an aspect of the environment inhabited by the organism; for reasons to be made clear, we can refer to „the aspect of the environment‟ as an „object‟, and name this particular type of object a „Stressor‟.
5
Bartley, M. (2004). Health Inequality: an introduction to theories, concepts, and methods. Cambridge, Polity Press. pp. 80-1 6 Damasio, A. R. (2000). The feeling of what happens : body and emotion in the making of consciousness. London, W. Heinemann. p. 55
 The biological organism is in this case a „human being‟, one of „us‟ at the most fundamental physiological, emotional, cognitive and existential level. We would thus lose something of the substantive implications of this perspective if we were to refer to this organism in a way which implies an ontological „otherness‟ („they‟, „it‟). Instead, as „we‟ are „the organism‟, the above passage, a technical description of human physiology, begins to assume a tragic quality: We are made conscious of our emotional responses to different social situations, in order that we can make decisions about how to act in order to move away from the negative environmental aspects – „Stressors‟, in this case – and towards positive environmental aspects. However, we exist in a social environment where such Stressors are all-pervasive. Our awareness of our emotions means that we know we should move away from the Stressors, but the environment is such that we cannot. The use of the first-person plural within the above discussion is intended to suggest both what may be at stake, and what might be gained, from temporarily adopting an organism-centred perspective to social and societal issues, as opposed to the more intuitive individual- and group-centred perspectives we are likely to adopt in every-day life. Once the behaviours and sensations of the individual, and the sense of being an individual, are understood to be largely explicable in terms of, and often appear consequences of, the properties and functions of the organism, then the extent to which being able to make a gestalt shift in perspective, from that of the individual, to that of the organism, can aid our understanding of social, sociological, and societal phenomena becomes clearer.
2.3 The Individual and the Organism To help make this transition, I will briefly consider an apparently esoteric and abstract problem usually addressed only by philosophers: the nature (and the notion) of „self‟. In Bertrand Russell‟s History of Western Philosophy, when discussing the French philosopher René Descartes‟ famous assertion “Cogito, ergo sum” (“I think, therefore I am”), he suggests the statement is problematic because „I‟ (the „–o‟ part of „cogito‟) is undefined. Instead, suggests Russell, the statement should simply assert that “there are thoughts”.7 William James, a few decades earlier, made a similar observation within his book The Principles of Psychology. 8
7
Russell wrote that: Descartes’s indubitable facts are his own thoughts – using ‘thought’ in the widest possible sense. ‘I think’ is his ultimate premise. Here the word ‘I’ is really illegitimate; he ought to state
 It is astonishing what havoc is wrought in psychology by admitting at the outset apparently innocent suppositions that nevertheless contain a flaw. The bad consequences develop themselves later on, and are irredeemable, being woven through the whole texture of the work. … The only thing which psychology has a right to postulate at the outset is the fact of thinking itself, and that must first be taken up and analyzed. […] The first fact for us, then, as psychologists, is that thinking of some sort goes on. … If we could say in English „it thinks,‟ as we say „it rains‟ or „it blows,‟ we should be stating the fact most simply and with the minimum of assumption. As we cannot, we must simply that that thought goes on. If accepting the concept of self is more problematic than accepting the concept of thinking, then this raises a new question: What is it that is doing the thinking? The problem of what is thinking led Damasio to suggest the following: How do we ever know that we are seeing a given object? How do we become conscious in the full sense of the word? How is the sense of self in the act of knowing implanted in the mind? The way into a possible answer for the questions on self came only after I began seeing the problem of consciousness in terms of two key players, the organism and the object, and in terms of the relationships those players hold in the course of their natural interactions. The organism in question is that within which consciousness occurs; the object in question is any object that gets to be known in the consciousness process; and the relationships between organism and object are the contents of the knowledge we call consciousness. Seen in this perspective, consciousness consists of constructing knowledge about two facts: that the organism is involved in relation to some object, and that the object in the relation causes a change in the organism.9 When a change occurs within an organism, as a result of its relationship with a particular object, Damasio suggests the organism experiences an „image‟ of the object.
his ultimate premise in the form ‘there are thoughts’. The word ‘I’ is grammatically convenient, but does not describe a datum. Russell, B. (2000 [1946]). History of Western Philosophy. London, Routledge. p. 550 8 James, W. (1890). The Principles of Psychology. New York, Dover. pp. 224-5 9 Damasio, A. R. (2000). The feeling of what happens : body and emotion in the making of consciousness. London, W. Heinemann. pp. 19-20
 In this case, he uses the term „image‟ as a synonym for „mental pattern‟,10 and by this usage an „image‟ generalises beyond visual patterns, referring equally, for example, to tactile patterns (the sensation of being touched by a feather, or of a spider crawling along one‟s skin), auditory patterns (the sound of a police siren speeding away on a nearby street), olfactory patterns (the smell of roses or fetid gorgonzola), or any other form of sensory pattern. The organism does not have to be conscious of an image, or even be capable of consciousness, in order to experience the image, because an image of an object is simply the organism‟s subjective response to the object. (Therefore, if there were no organisms, there would be no objects, and no images.) There are some images that the organism is conscious of experiencing, and others that the organism is not. Whether subjectively experienced or not, biological correlates of images may – in theory, but not always in practice – be observed by third parties. Examples of such correlates include: changes in the skin‟s electrical conductance; changes in the form – pattern, shape, intensity, duration – of electromagnetic field surrounding the skull. From Damasio‟s perspective, all subjective mental images could, in theory, be shown to be perfectly correlated with a given objective pattern of biological activity.
2.4 Organisms and Boundaries The term „organism‟ itself is perhaps even more difficult, but at least as important, to understand. Most fundamentally, the concept of an „organism‟ (much like that of an „organisation‟, covered later in the thesis), is dependent upon “the definition of their boundary, the separation between that is in and what is out.”11 The structure of the organism is inside the boundary and the life of the organism is defined by the maintenance of internal states within the boundary. Singular individuality depends on the boundary.12 Damasio then states that “even when large variations occur in the environment that surrounds an organism, there is a dispositional arrangement in the organism‟s structure that modifies the inner workings of the organism.”13
10
Ibid. p. 317 Damasio, A. R. (1995). Descartes’ error : emotion, reason and the human brain. New York, Avon Books. pp. 135-6 12 Ibid., pp. 135-6 13 Ibid., pp. 135-6 11
 The dispositional arrangement ensures that the environmental variations do not cause a correspondingly large and excessive variation of activity within. When variations that trespass into a dangerous range are about to occur, they can be averted by some preemptive action; and where dangerous variations have already occurred, they can still be corrected by some appropriate action.14 The process by which a living system or organism comes to maintain its nonequilibrium stable state is called homeostasis. As Damasio explains: Homeostasis refers to the coordinated and largely automated physiological reactions required to maintain stable internal states in a living organism. Homeostasis describes the automatic regulation of temperature, oxygen concentration, or pH in your body.15 From this perspective, organisms are thus fundamentally a form of homeostatic system, which use a series of internal feedback loops to self-regulate and maintain a nonequilibrium stable state with respect to a variety of qualities that exist within the organism. Once the internal structure of the organism loses its functional integrity, and can no longer maintain the differential boundary between itself and that which surrounds it, then, by this definition, it ceases to be alive.
2.5 Organisms as Abstract Machines In asserting that the organism functions as a homeostatic system, the claim is implicitly being made that the organism represents a particular form of – to use a term Manuel De Landa attributes to Giles Deleuze and Felix Guattari – „abstract machine.‟16 This may be thought of, heuristically, as a formal specification of a set of relationships between components, but not of the materials out of which the components are constructed. For example, both an unopened bottle of wine and an unopened tin can may be thought of as different instantiations of the same abstract machine „closed container‟, which defines a relationship –that may be formally specified topologically - between three components: „contents‟, „boundary‟, and „outside‟. These abstract machine components may be thought to „exist‟ to the extent that one can perform isomorphic „mapping‟ between substrates: „the glass is to the bottle as the tin is to the can‟, „the wine is to the bottle as 14
Damasio, A. R. (2000). The feeling of what happens : body and emotion in the making of consciousness. London, W. Heinemann. pp. 135-6 15 Damasio, A. R. (1995). Descartes’ error : emotion, reason and the human brain. New York, Avon Books.., pp. 40-41 16 See De Landa, M. (2000). A Thousand Years of Nonlinear History. New York, Swerve. esp. pp. 57-70
 the baked beans are to the can‟, and so on. The names given to enterprises which attempt to describe and understand this form of abstract machine, the homeostatic system, include „systems theory‟, „control theory‟ and „cybernetics‟. To recapitulate: from a cybernetics perspective, a system could be considered to be „alive‟ if it manages, through feedback loops and mechanisms within its internal structure, to maintain a non-equilibrium stable state, with regard to a set of definable and measurable properties, and with respect to that which is external to it (the environment). The „equilibrium‟ refers to the normal state of the environment with respect to those properties by which the „living system‟ and environment differ. Functionally, „the boundary‟, by which the living system or organism is defined, refers to a significant and systematic difference between the conditions of a given range of properties - moisture content, pH levels, electrical activity and so on - between that which is outside (the environment) and that which is inside (the organism).
2.6 Objects and Emotions An object is an aspect of the environment that has been differentiated from others by an organism. Even without consciousness, the organism can objectify aspects of the environment by producing systematically different responses to different environmental aspects (i.e. produce images of the objects). For example, towards some aspects of the environment, the organism may shrink, and to others it may swell. In this example the former environmental aspect would be, from the perspective of the organism, a „shrinkinducing‟ object, whereas the latter would be a „swell-inducing‟ object. At the most basic level, an emotion is a response to an object. Within the hypothesised organism above, shrinking and swelling are, effectively, emotions. Damasio asserts that “emotions are part and parcel of the regulations we call homeostasis.”17 In the ShrinkSweller, one may presume, the emotional responses of shrinking and swelling may allow better regulation of core temperature when the external temperature of the environment decrease, in the cases of shrinking, or increases rapidly, in the case of swelling.
17
Damasio, A. R. (2000). The feeling of what happens : body and emotion in the making of consciousness. London, W. Heinemann., p. 41
 2.7 Feelings and Self Decision-making requires a conscious, reasoning self. Damasio suggests the faculties for reason and consciousness emerge atop a biological hierarchy of increasingly complex biological and regulatory processes, as shown in






Figure 1: Levels of Life Regulation



Figure 2.1 below.
Figure 2.1 Levels of Life Regulation (Redrawn from Damasio 2000, p. 55)
Note that the arrows of causal influence, from highest to lowest level, run in both directions. In order for an emotion to become a feeling, there needs to exist the biological machinery for signalling, in one part of the organism, that an emotion is occurring in another part. Such signals can then be relayed to the non-emotive part of the organism, and the appropriate emotional response generated. To continue with the example of the Shrink-Sweller, a shrink-inducing object may induce an emotional response through direct contact with one side of the organism. This change in the internal state of that side of the organism could then, through a sequence of internal causal influence, lead to a similar emotional response within the rest of the
 organism. If the sequence of internal causal influence is systematic in its behaviour in response to the change in the emotional state within an aspect of the organism, then this causal sequence functions as a signalling mechanism within the organism. This internal signalling, in response to emotional change, is the core quality of feeling. This particular pattern of signalling responses, resulting from the organism‟s contact with the object, is what Damasio refers to as an image; the Shrink-Sweller organism thus has an „image‟ of shrink-inducing objects, and an „image‟ of swell-inducing objects.
2.8 Centralisation and the Proto-self An early prerequisite for the sense of self is the emergence of a complex, holistic system of emotional identification and signalling within the organism. This is the function of the central nervous system. With internal sensory centralisation, the emotional responses – complex, unconscious behaviours – of the entire organism can come to be coordinated, and where such coordination of activity is evolutionary advantageous such trends towards sensory centralisation develop. The sense of self depends upon the capacity of the organism to behave coherently as a single entity, and respond systematically, effectively and in a coordinated fashion to changes in any of its aspects. When an organism becomes centrally connected in this way, it becomes meaningful to consider the organism to be an object with respect to itself. (To recall, an „object‟ has here been defined as something which may have systematic influence upon the organism. With a central nervous system, which relays changes in the emotional state of any aspect of the organism, the organism comes to yield a coherent, systematic, unified response upon itself.)
2.9 Emotions and Decision-making Given the above discussion, I am now in a better position to start to understand the process of decision-making, and the relationship between decision-making and emotions. This relationship should not be underestimated. As Damasio states, “emotion is integral to the processes of reasoning and decision making, for worse and for better.”18 From this perspective, the faculty of decision-making relates intimately to the prediction, generation, and evaluation of differing emotional states under a variety of hypothesised environmental circumstances. The capacity to reason is thus ultimately dependent upon, and in service to, the capacity to know and compare feelings. The 18
Ibid., p. 41
 faculty for reason thus does not, and cannot, exist without emotion (which itself cannot exist without complex life). As Damasio states, “the engines of reason still require emotion, which means that the controlling power of reason is often modest.”19 Thoughts, elements of the reasoning process, come to generate dispositional memories of how the organism, the biological predicate of the self, tends to respond to a given object. There is no such thing as pure perception of an object within a sensory channel … They perceive an object … the organism requires both specialized sensory signals and signals from the adjustment of the body, which are necessary for perception to occur. […] The statement also holds true when you are simply thinking of an object rather than actually perceiving it in the world outside your organism. … The records we hold of the objects and events that we once perceived include the motor adjustments we made to obtain the perception in the first place and also include the emotional reactions we had then. [Emphasis added].
[E]ven when we
“merely” think about an object, we tend to reconstruct memories not just of a shape or color but also of the perceptual engagement the objects required and of the accompanying emotional reactions, regardless of how slight. … [T]he images you form in your mind always signal to the organism its own engagement with the business of making images and evoke some emotional reactions. You simply cannot escape the affectation of your organism, motor and emotional most of all, that is part and parcel of having a mind.20 […] Now let us turn to the case of an object that is not actually present but has, rather, been committed to memory. According to my framework, the memory of that object has been stored in dispositional form. Dispositions are records which are dormant and implicit rather than active and explicit, as images are. Those dispositional memories of an object that was once actually perceived include not only records of the sensory aspects of the object … but also records of the motor adjustments that necessarily accompanies the gathering of the sensory signals;
19 20
Ibid., p. 58 Ibid. pp. 147-8, emphasis in original unless otherwise stated
 moreover the memories also contain records of the obligate emotional reaction to the object. As a consequence, when we recall an object, when we allow dispositions to make their implicit information explicit, we retrieve not just sensory data but also accompanying motor and emotional data. When we recall an object, we recall not just sensory characteristics of an actual object but the past reactions of the organism to that object.21 The image of the object is re-imagined, and through this the dispositional tendencies of the organism in response to the object come to be remembered (and thus to some extent involuntarily „performed‟).
2.10 The As-if-body-loop If mindfulness and consciousness perform, ultimately, the function of selecting from a limited range of potential behaviours, a limited proportion of the time, it follows that the vast majority of one‟s behaviours are not consciously determined at all. Instead, they exist as unconscious, predetermined patterns of behaviour, of varying levels of complexity, in response to particular „stimuli‟. If this process of remembering were to continually involve the entire body, the process could be expensive, in terms of both time and other resources (because, in this case, deciding would require rapid and substantial alteration of the body‟s electro-chemical and physiological functioning). Thus, in order to make the process of reasoning „cheaper‟, faculties of the brain develop which simulate the responses of the rest of the body to a given object. Thoughts entering this „as-if-body-loop‟, as Damasio calls it, thus induce the same kinds of response within the periphery of the brain, from the perspective of the decisionmaking aspects of the brain, as would comparable signals from the brain to the rest of the body. Much of the brain thus behaves as a kind of emotional simulator, a cognitive laboratory under which various what-if scenarios, involving different relationships between objects, may be compared in order to assess their likely effects upon the organism.
2.11 From Memories and Dispositions to Habits and Habitus The previous sections have described, from a neurological perspective, how decisions are made. In doing so, it has illustrated the extent to which unconscious processes predominate both our responses to, and understanding of, our environment, and our conscious decisions to aspects thereof. Understanding individual and group behaviours 21
Ibid. pp. 160-1, emphasis added
 may often be more a matter of understanding the decisions people don‟t make than the decisions that they do. Recall from Figure 2.1 that the majority of processes performed by the human organism are below the level of consciousness, and also that the causal arrows – from „high‟ to „low‟ processes – go in both directions. This makes sense if one thinks about it: bring to one‟s mind and linger upon a vivid memory of a time one was scared, and within seconds this act of „high reason‟ has permeated downwards through the levels, rekindling a sense of the feeling of fear (initially just within the as-if-body loop), then inducing a genuine whole-body emotion, which in turn leads to changes in the performance of aspects of one‟s basic physiology (faster breathing, greater perspiration, and so on); the low level physiological change may then permeate back upwards, heightening the strength of the emotional response, which then amplifies the feeling (which now occurs throughout the entire body, rather than just within the as-if-body loop), which in turn makes the fearful memory even more vivid. Conscious acts also enter the realm of the unconscious by generating dispositional tendencies within the organism; more colloquially, these may be termed „habits‟, and may be thought of as complex patterns of behaviour acquired through experiences. Although the division is far from absolute, the capacity to acquire habits or dispositions is something that separates people from most other types of organism. As William James wrote: Man22 is born with a tendency to do more things than his has ready-made arrangements for in his nerve-centres. Most of the performances of other animals are automatic. But in him the number of them is so enormous, that most of them must be the fruit of painful study. If practice did not make perfect, nor habit economize the expense of nervous and muscular energy, he would therefore be in a sorry plight.23 Being „too conscious‟, therefore, of the vast and complex array of repertoires one needs to perform in order to function on a day-to-day basis, is likely to severely impede the extent to which one can perform such repertoires, and so function effectively. And as
22
The reader will hopefully forgive the archaic use of male nouns and pronouns to refer to both males and females. James, W. (1890). The Principles of Psychology. New York, Dover. p. 113
 James then explained: “habit diminishes the conscious attention with which our acts are performed.24” He continues: One may state this abstractly thus: If an act requires for its execution a chain, A, B, C, D, E,F,G, etc., of successive nervous events, then in the first performances of the action the conscious will must choose each of these events from a number of wrong alternatives that tend to present themselves; but habit soon brings it about that each event calls up its own appropriate successor without any alternative offering itself, and without any references to the conscious will, until at last the whole chain, A, B, C, D, E, F, G, rattles itself off as soon as A occurs, just as if A and the rest of the chain were fused into a continuous stream.25 As a tangible example of the above process, James offered the following illustration: The writer well remembers how, on revisiting Paris after ten years‟ absence, and, findings himself in the street in which for one winter he had attended school, he lost himself in a brown study, from which he was awakened by finding himself upon the stairs which led to the apartment in a house many streets away in which he had lived during that earlier time, and to which his steps from the school had then habitually led. We all of us have a definite routine manner of performing certain daily offices […]. Our lower centres know the order of these movements, and show their knowledge by their „surprise‟ if the objects are altered so as to oblige the movement to be made in a different way. But our higher thought-centres know hardly anything about the matter.26 Just as James‟ discussion of habits can be called upon to highlight the relationship between „higher functions‟ of the human organism and every-day individual behaviours and experiences, so Pierre Bourdieu‟s concept of habitus can be used to link individual habits with social groups. As an editor‟s introduction to an volume of Bourdieu‟s essays describes the concept: The habitus is a set of dispositions which incline agents to act and react in certain ways. The dispositions generate practices, perceptions and attitudes which are „regular‟ without being consciously co-ordinated or governed by any „rule‟. The dispositions generate practices, perceptions and attitudes which are
Ibid. p.114, emphasis in the original Ibid. p. 114, emphasis added Ibid., p. 115, emphasis added
 „regular‟ without consciously co-ordinated or governed by any „rule‟. The dispositions which constitute the habitus are inculcated, structured, durable, generative and transposable – features that each deserve a brief explanation. Dispositions are acquired through a gradual process of inculcation in which early childhood experiences are particularly important. Through a myriad of mundane processes of training and learning … the individual acquires a set of dispositions which literally mould the body and become second nature. The dispositions produced thereby are also structured in the sense that they unavoidably reflect the social conditions within which they are acquired. … [T]he similarities and differences that characterize the social conditions of existence of individuals will be reflected in the habitus, which may be relatively homogeneous across individuals from similar backgrounds. Structured dispositions are […] ingrained in the body in such a way that they endure through the life history of the individual, operating in a way that is preconscious and hence not readily amenable to conscious reflection and modification [and] are generative and transposable in the sense that they are capable of generating a multiplicity of practices and perceptions in fields other than those in which they were originally acquired. As a durably installed set of dispositions, the habitus tends to generate practices and perceptions, works and appreciations, which concur with the conditions of existence of which the habitus is itself the product.27 In any case, the extent to which broader social factors systematically shape individual habits and dispositions, and the extent to which the capacity for persons to embody dispositions is dependent upon the qualities of the „human organism‟ – what we have in common with, and what makes us (mostly) distinct from, other forms of „organism‟ – should now be clear. A rudimentary understanding of physiology and neurobiology can thus be very useful in helping to understand problems that occur at the levels of human individuals, groups of individuals, and larger scale groups of groups.
2.12 Conclusion: Sketching an Abstract Machine This chapter has considered organisms both in the specific sense of the human organism, and in a more general sense, of „the organism‟ as a particular form of
Thompson, J. B. (1992). Editor’s Introduction. Language and Symbolic Power. J. B. Thompson. Oxford, Polity Press: 1-31. pp. 12-13, emphasis in original.
 „abstract machine‟. From the more general perspective, the „the human organism‟ represents just one out of many possible instantiations. To what extent could „the organism as abstract machine‟ – a particular, scientific way of thinking about types of relationship between types of elements – be usefully extended beyond the realm of biological phenomena, and used to gain insight into sociological phenomena? Based on the above, here is a preliminary, and highly speculative, list of defining qualities of the organism-as-abstract-machine: 1. A salient and meaningful definition of a boundary, demarcating an „inside‟ from an „outside‟, which does predictive work in allowing observers to infer systematic differentials between the conditions on either side of the boundary with respect to a number of specifiable properties. 2. Organisms may be of markedly differing sizes, but are composed of (for the purposes of analysis) irreducible elements known as „cells‟. 3. The overall purpose of an organism is homeostasis: the maintenance of the boundary defining inside from outside, and thus the establishment of an obdurate coherence in structure. 4. As the size of (the number of cells contained within) the organism increases, so there is a tendency for two concurrent changes to occur with respect to the relationship between, and the performances of, individual cells: Firstly, for the degree of organism centralisation – the extent to which experiences encountered by aspects of the organism affect the entire organism – to increase; and secondly, for increasing functional specialisation of individual cells, with a cell‟s particular function determined primarily by its relationship with other cells, and with the organism boundary. These two concurrent properties may be thought heuristically as defining the organism‟s complexity. 5. Within more complex organisms, centralisation allows more rapid information transfer between cells throughout the organism, and thus greater co-ordination of the organism as a whole. With centralisation, an internal command-and-control structure emerges. As the possible variety of specialised functions undertaken by any particular cell within an organism increases, so some cells (in the biological organism, those in the brain/central nervous system) take on the exclusive task of information processing, and others information transfer. However, the information reaching these central control cells is heavily filtered
 and mediated by intermediate cells, and only an abstracted fraction of the information available to the „source‟ cells. 6. Within more complex organisms, groups of cells may operate (and be understood more effectively) as organism sub-systems; each cell within the cluster may well exhibit relatively simple behaviour, but collectively exhibit more complex behaviour whose internal and external influences are better understood by thinking of the cluster as a single unit with a given set of properties, than by considering each constituent cell individually. 7. The vast majority of cells (and sub-systems) tend to exhibit both „autonomous‟ and „directed‟ modes of operation. When adjacent cells (or sub-systems) are not signalling (transferring relevant information) that a particular cell (or subsystem) should do otherwise, the cell (or sub-system) has a default form of operation. When particular signals are received, however, the operations performed change in a systematic manner. This list is by no means exhaustive or systematic, but hopefully highlights which types of element and relationship need to be identified in non-biological phenomena in order that the abstracted ontology of „the organism‟ can be usefully applied. Just as this chapter has considered the unintuitively small, so the next chapter will consider the unintuitively large: processes of societal emergence and change that played out over large sections of the globe, and over thousands of years.

title: MA: Critical Theory and Cultural Studies
text:
Dissertation
The MA dissertation, “Richard Rorty and the New Left,” is not currently available in the digital archive. A physical copy may exist and is being sought. If recovered, it will be digitised and added here.






Looking for this document



The dissertation examined Richard Rorty’s pragmatist philosophy in relation to the New Left political tradition. If you are reading this and happen to have a copy (perhaps from the University of Nottingham library), please get in touch.

title: MA: Critical Theory and Cultural Studies
text:
Context
The MA in Critical Theory and Cultural Studies was completed between the BEng in Electronic Engineering (2003) and the PhD in Sociology and Human Geography (2005–2009). It represents a significant intellectual pivot — from engineering through continental philosophy and cultural theory towards the social sciences.
An extended research document from this transition period, “Simulating Geographies of Homelessness and Worklessness” (April 2006), survives in the archive. It represents an early direction for the PhD and is presented as an optional appendix under the PhD section.

title: Chapter 3: Empires, Elites and States: Some Theories of Very Large Scale Social Phenomena
text:
Source: Pages 40–58 of MintonThesis.pdf (September 2009). Text extracted from PDF; figures extracted directly as images.

3 Empires, Elites and States: Some Theories of Very Large Scale Social Phenomena 3.1 Introduction Within the previous chapter, I considered biological organisms in order both to understand some of the biological processes that underlie everyday experiences; and also to provide a conceptual device – organism-as-abstract-machine – that one may attempt to apply to understanding other phenomena. This chapter will draw more from the latter aspect of the previous chapter than the former. The suggestion will be made that very-large scale social structures share a number of striking similarities with verysmall scale biological structures: these include a lack of prior intentional design; a (tautologically-defined) homeostatic purpose; and a co-emergence of functional specialisation and hierarchy with structure complexity. This does not mean, however, that the contents of this chapter will not speak to, and attempt to explain aspects of, everyday lived experiences as chapter two did. Instead, one may argue that the results of the processes described here have shaped our experiences to an overwhelming extent. Furthermore, the processes described in this chapter, like those described in the previous chapters, are prerequisites for the existence of the kinds of social, economic and political issues discussed in later chapters. As with the previous chapter, in order to maintain consistency in terminology and focus, the chapter will be based around a single account; in this case, Jared Diamond‟s account of the emergence and dominance of complex societies over the last 10,000 years.28 The account offered within this chapter will attempt to remain close to the original source, but will: a) more explicitly suggest the potential application of an „evolutionary‟ mode of thinking to understanding the processes described; b) highlight the idea that modern societies are variations of, rather than alternatives to, earlier societies, and may be thought of as part of an unbroken continuation of predecessors; and c) emphasise the holistic coherence, the extent to which the whole should be considered qualitatively different from the sum of its parts, of the social structures described.
28
Diamond, J. (1998). Guns, Germs and Steel: A short history of everybody for the last 13,000 years. London, Vintage.; in particular pp. 265-292, a chapter called ‘From Egalitarianism to Kleptocracy’, which is based on seventeen source materials written, primarily, by anthropologists in the 1960s and 1970s.
 3.2 Evolution and Social Processes The psychologist and social science methodologist Donald Campbell suggests that, when applying evolutionary thinking to cultural phenomena, the terms „blind variation‟ and „selective retention‟ be used instead of „natural selection‟. The reasons for this are two-fold: firstly, because these terms describe the two basic mechanisms that together constitute a natural selective process (but that may also exist separately); and secondly, to emphasise – by using the word „blind‟ – that variation need not be completely random in order for the process to operate.29 In adopting this terminology, it becomes clearer that valid applications of the concept are those where one can identify the existence of, and interplay between, these two mechanisms, and so one is not restricted to those instantiated in similar materials or operating at similar scales as those in which they were originally identified and applied. Another useful concept with which Campbell is credited is that of „downward causation‟. As Campbell described it: Where natural selection operates through life and death at a higher level of organization, the laws of the higher level selective system determine in part the distribution of lower level events and substances. Description of an intermediate-level phenomenon is not completed by describing its possibility and implementation in lower level terms. Its presence, prevalence, or distribution […] will often require reference to laws at a higher level of organization as well. […] [A]ll processes at the lower levels of a hierarchy are restrained by, and act in conformity to, the laws of the higher levels.30 As the following discussion will show, the survival of the individual has consistently and extensively depended on the qualities of the social structure within which he or she is born and embedded.
3.3 Food, Social Structure, and Societal Complexity The most basic version of the argument presented in this chapter is indicated in Figure 3.1. On both the left and right sides of the figure, a form of social structure (rectangle) is linked reciprocally to a means of acquiring food (oval). The social structure „Nomadic 29
See Campbell, D. T. (1969). “Variation and Selective Retention in Socio-Cultural Evolution.” General Systems 14: 69-&. 30 P. 4 of Campbell, D. T. (1990). Levels of Organization, Downward Causation, and the Selection-Theory Approach to Evolutionary Epistemology. Theories of the Evolution of Knowing. E. Tobach. Hillsdale, NJ, Lawrence Erlbaum: 1-17. Of course, the idea - that in order to understand the part, one must understand the whole – is not new, and is often associated with, amongst others, Emile Durkheim.
 Living‟ is paired with the „Hunter-Gathering‟ means of provisioning; and the social structure „Sedentary living‟ is paired with „agriculture‟ as a means of provisioning.






Figure 1: Two ‘modes of living’



Figure 3.1 Two ‘modes of living’. Rectangles denote social structures; ovals denote means of acquiring food. Thickness of lines indicates degree of association. Arrows indicate causality. Note that causal path exists linking hunter-gathering to agriculture, but not agriculture to hunter-gathering
The reasons for these two particular types of pairing are relatively simple: „huntergathering‟ requires moving from place to place, as hunting and gathering in a single area would lead to a degradation in its feeding capacity; acquiring food in this way thus needs a willingness to relocate, frequently, to more hospitable places. Agriculture, by contrast, requires a long period of intensive cultivation, development, and maintenance of an environment in order for it to produce food; this requires staying close to the areas being cultivated, in order to be able to tend to it sufficiently. The item labelled „Proto-Agriculture‟ provides a link between the former and the latter means of provisioning. „Proto-Agriculture‟, to use Campbell‟s term, may be thought of as a „blind variation‟ of traditional hunter-gathering practices. Diamond suggests humans, like other animals, may have accidentally cultivated and domesticated a number of species as an incidental by-product of finding and eating suitable foods: Countless […] plants have fruits adapted to being eaten and dispersed by particular species of animals. Just as strawberries are adapted to birds, so acorns are adapted to squirrels, mangoes to bats, and some sedges to ants. That fulfils part of our definition of plant domestication, as the genetic modification of an ancestral plant in ways that make it more useful to consumers. But no one would serious describe this evolutionary process as domestication, because birds and
 bats and other animal consumers don‟t fulfil the other part of the definition: they don‟t consciously grow plants. In the same way, the early unconscious stages of crop evolution from wild plants consisted of plants evolving in ways that attracted humans to eat and disperse their fruit without yet intentionally growing them. Human latrines, like those of aardvarks, may have been a testing ground of the first unconscious crop breeders.31 The act of gathering suitable foods from a wide area then eating them thus has the inadvertent effect of concentrating such foods in a much smaller area. These food-rich areas are thus likely to be visited again later, causing them to become even more „refined‟. Choosing to further cultivate such areas, by intentionally bringing and planting the most edible and nutritious plants, and removing less edible plants, can thus be seen to emerge as a subtle, „blind variation‟ of a standard hunter-gathering pattern. However, and as indicated by the dashed lines in Figure 3.1, the wholesale adoption of an agricultural pattern is not necessarily an inevitable transition. As Diamond writes: Formerly, all people on earth were Hunter-Gatherers. Why did any of them adopt food production at all? […] From our modern perspective […] the drawbacks of being a hunter-gatherer appear so obvious [but] […] [I]n reality, only for today‟s affluent First world citizens, who don‟t actually do the work of raising food themselves, does food production (by remote agribusiness) mean less physical work, more comfort, freedom from starvation, and a longer expected lifetime. Most peasant farmers and herders, who constitute the great majority of the world‟s actual food producers, aren‟t necessarily better off than hunter-gatherers. Time budget studies show that they may spend more rather than fewer hours per day at work than hunger-gatherers do. Archaeologists have demonstrated that the first farmers in many areas were smaller and less well nourished, suffered from more serious diseases, and died on the average at a younger age than the hunter-gatherers they replaced. If those first farmers could have foreseen the consequences of adopting food production, they might not have opted to do so. Why, unable to foresee the result, did they nevertheless make that choice?32
31
Diamond, J. (1998). Guns, Germs and Steel: A short history of everybody for the last 13,000 years. London, Vintage. , pp. 116-7 32 Ibid. pp. 104-5
 Diamond uses the term „choice‟ somewhat ironically, as he suggests that the transition towards agriculture was seldom made consciously, and tended to emerge as a series of piecemeal changes with provisioning practices likely to involve an admixture of both agricultural and foraging aspects. Although any particular group, at this „dabbling‟ stage, may vacillate between greater and lesser reliance on agricultural practices, the longer-term transition has been one way. Diamond suggests four factors were important in first generating, then exacerbating, and then locking in this change: 1. The “decline in the availability of wild foods.” 2. An “increased availability of domesticable wild plants”, occurring concurrently with the first factor. 3. The “cumulative development of technologies on which food production would eventually depend – technologies for collecting, processing, and storing wild foods.” 4. The “two-way link between the rise in human population density and the rise in food production.”33 These four factors are unlikely to have been independent from one another: one can imagine a steeper decline in the availability of wild foods (factor one) leading to increasing reliance on agriculture, and increasing effort being put into finding domesticable wild plants, leading to increased availability thereof (factor two), as well as more emphasis on developing means of collecting, processing and storing such foods (factor three). Conversely, increasing agriculture (following from factors two and three) can be understood to reduce the heterogeneity of the environment, and thus reduce the availability of wild foods (factor one). Bearing the above in mind, however, the focus within this chapter is on factor four: this „two-way link‟ between human population density and the rise in food production. Increased population density does not simply lead to existing forms of social structure being reproduced on ever larger (or more compressed) scales, but to qualitative shifts in the form of social structure that operate. The “size of the regional population”, Diamond writes, “is the strongest single predictor of societal complexity”34 As the population density grows, so the society becomes more complex.
33 34
Ibid. pp. 110-1 Ibid. p. 284, emphasis added.
 Intensified food production and societal complexity stimulate each other, by autocatalysis. That is population growth leads to societal complexity […] while societal complexity in turn leads to intensified food production and thereby to population growth. Complex centralized societies are uniquely capable of organizing public works […], long-distance trade […], and activities of different groups of economic specialists […] All these capabilities of centralized societies have fostered intensified food production and hence population growth throughout history. 35 Autocatalysis, in this case, describes a process of positive feedback between two components, in which the rate of increase in the magnitude of each of the components is a positive function of the magnitude of the other component. Another term for autocatalysis is reciprocal causation.
3.4 Stages of Societal Complexity Diamond posits four broad stages of societal complexity: bands, tribes, chiefdoms and states.36 Diamond summarises the main features, and differences between, these stages in a table, which is reproduced as table 3.1 below.
35
Ibid. p. 285, emphasis added Diamond credits the typology to two books: Service, E. (1962). Primitive Social Organization. New York, Random House, Service, E. (1975). Origins of the State and Civilization. New York, Norton. Each of the classificatory stages is heuristic, and in reality levels of societal complexity exist along a continuum, rather than as easily identifiable discrete stages: just as there is no definite point at which day becomes night, there is no definite dividing line between a social group organised as a band, one organised as a tribe, one organised as a chiefdom, and one organised as a state. 36
