Part Two: The Growth of Elites
Introduction
In this section, I will discuss some theories on how social hierarchies came to emerge and be perpetuated across generations.  This section proceeds largely as a summary of a chapter within Jared Diamond’s book Guns, Germs and Steel {Diamond, 1998 #13}[Ref], called “From Egalitarianism to Kleptocracy”. {Diamond, 1998 #14}[Ref] However, it will also include a number of relatively novel associations between the theories expounded within this chapter, and other theorists, as well as positing a simple model, developing from Abram de Swaan’s In Care of the State {de Swaan, 1988 #15} [ref], in order to link Diamond’s discussions more explicitly to more contemporary issues of social welfare.  
Population, elites, and societal complexity
Diamond suggests four broad stages of societal complexity: bands, tribes, chiefdoms and states. Each stage is significantly more complex in its social organisation than the previous. The “size of the regional population”, Diamond writes,* “is the strongest single predictor of societal complexity”* {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 284, my emphasis]. As the population grows, so the society becomes more complex. 
Intensified food production and societal complexity stimulate each other, by autocatalysis. That is population growth leads to societal complexity … while societal complexity in turn leads to intensified food production and thereby to population growth. Complex centralized societies are uniquely capable of organizing public works …, long-distance trade …, and activities of different groups of economic specialists … All these capabilities of centralized societies have fostered intensified food production and hence population growth throughout history. {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 285, my emphasis]
Autocatalysis
Autocatalysis, a term borrowed from chemistry, is in systems theory a particular, simple form of positive feedback, in which small changes in one component, X, induce small changes in component Y, which in turn induces further small changes in component X, which in turn induces further small changes in component Y…. and so on, until some new stable state is reached by both components. Another term for autocatalysis is reciprocal causation, and the general features of such a process are best expressed diagrammatically:
Figure 2: Reciprocal Causation
X
Y




Types of Social Group
The simplest social structure Diamond proposes is the band, comprising just a few dozen members:
Bands lack many institutions that we take for granted in our own society. They have no permanent single base of residence. The band’s land is used jointly by the whole group, instead of being partitioned among subgroups or individuals. There is no regular economic specialization, except by age and sex: all able-bodied individuals forage for food. There are no formal institutions, such as laws, police, and treaties, to resolve conflicts within and between bands. {Diamond, 1998 #14}[pp. 268-7]
Diamond continues:
Band organisation is often described as “Egalitarian”: there is no formalized social stratification into upper and lower classes, no formalized or hereditary leadership, and no formalized monopolies of information and decision making. However, the term “egalitarian” should not be taken to mean that all band members are equal in prestige and contribute equally to decisions. Rather, the term merely means that any band “leadership” is informal and acquired through qualifies such as personality, strength, intelligence, and fighting skills. {Diamond, 1998 #14}[pp. 269]
One level of social complexity above is the tribe, with typical numbers in the hundreds. These differ from the bands by “usually having fixed settlements.” {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 270] 
[A] tribe also differs [from a band] in that it consists of more than one formally recognized kinship group, termed clans, which exchange marriage partners. Land belongs to a particular clan, not to the whole tribe. However, the number of people in a tribe is still low enough that everyone knows everyone else by name and relationships. {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 271]
Within both bands and tribes: 
Their economy is based on reciprocal exchanges between individuals of families, rather than on a redistribution of tribute paid to some central authority. Economic specialization is slight: full-time crafts specialists are lacking, and every able-bodied adult … participates in growing, gathering, or hunting food. {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 272]
Chiefdoms, with typical group sizes in the thousands, are the next level of societal complexity:
The most distinctive economic feature of chiefdoms was their shift from reliance solely on the reciprocal exchanges characteristic of bands and tribes, by which A gives B a gift while expecting that B at some unspecified future time will give a gift of comparable value to A. … While continuing reciprocal exchanges and without marketing or money, chiefdoms developed an additional new system termed a redistributive economy. A simple example would involve a chief receiving wheat and gradually fiving it out again in the months between harvests. {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p.275]
A Note concerning Mauss, Bourdieu and Diamond
The categories Diamond suggests are heuristic, and in a sense arbitrary: there is no objective point at which a tribe becomes a chiefdom. It is perhaps for this reason that even this ‘distinctive economic feature’ of the redistributive economy can appear quite like any other, simpler, reciprocal exchange. The difference, however, is that in the redistributive economy there exists a clear and systematic hierarchical relationship between the giver and receiver of the gift. In the purely reciprocal economy of the egalitarian tribe, the function of the exchange is to enable the sharing of risk amongst group members: A may have experienced a period of bad luck, but meanwhile B has been lucky and achieved a surplus of a necessary good. B exchanges the surplus on condition that, when the tables are turned, A will reciprocate. This ensures both that neither A nor B become significantly richer than one another, and also that – so long as both A and B do not simultaneously come upon hard times – neither experiences such material privation as to endanger life. 
When the relationship between giver and receiver is unequal, however, the giving and receiving of gifts can have another function: to make the poorer member a debtor and the richer member a creditor, thus further reinforcing the unequal structure of the relationship to the advantage of the richer member. In providing a gift to a subordinate member of such great value that the subordinate will never be able to repay, the superordinate ensures that their social structural advantage becomes permanent. By having ‘in his pockets’ a large number of indebted subordinates, the superordinate may then be able to benefit from a kind of obligatory economy of scale, such that subordinates, in an attempt to pay off their obligation, relinquish goods and perform services in exchange for lower returns than they would demand of someone of equal stature. The superordinate can then call upon the cheap pool of resources the debtors provide in order to ensnare further poor members in a trap of chronic debt. 

Within sociology and anthropology, this type of practice has been well chronicled by both Emile Durkheim’s nephew, Marcel Mauss, and by Pierre Bourdieu. Both Mauss and Bourdieu emphasised the importance of understanding the *relational* characteristics of social groups in understanding sociological phenomena, and their anthropological research – in Mauss’ case within *The Gift* {Mauss, 2000 #16}[Ref] and in Bourdieu’s case within his *Outline of a Theory of Practice* {Bourdieu, 1977 #17} and *Logic of Practice {Bourdieu, 1990 #18} *[Ref] -  have both detailed methods through which social inequality becomes reinforced through the practice of gift-giving. Bourdieu used the term ‘symbolic violence’ to refer to inequality-reinforcing ensnaring social logic behind the *prima facia *benevolent activity of gift-giving from higher to lower tiers of hierarchical members. Mauss used a Northern Native American term ‘potlatch’ to both epitomise and refer to a variety of practices and complex ceremonies of frenzied gift exchange between tribal members which, though apparently benevolent and spontaneous, were in fact calculated and competitive attempts to ensnare subordinates and beat rivals in a battle to assert power and authority over others. [Ref? Quote?]

Though these types of economic exchanges might be thought of as ‘reciprocal’, and the groups involved may be described as ‘tribes’, within Diamond’s typology these would instead be examples of *redistributive* economic systems within *chiefdoms. *Difference in terminology should not obscure the fact that these classical sociological and anthropological accounts complement rather than contradict Diamond’s own descriptions.
The Growth of Inequality
As the society becomes more complex the level of inequality within society tends to increase: a few individuals come to have disproportionate material, power and influence over everyone else. One reason for this, which is often overlooked because of its apparent banality, is that in very simple societies there are simply not enough surplus resources for it to be possible for some individuals to subsist off others.  A surplus of food is required in order for there to be any significant form of specialisation within a society. As well as technical specialists, including craftspeople and inventors, a surplus of food also allows military specialists – a dedicated army and police force – and administrative specialists to emerge. Diamond suggests that one way in which elites can maintain their positions of power over other members of society is to placate commoners by effectively serving the function of administrative specialists. This requires finding solutions to a variety of problems which result from the larger populations of the society. These include:

The problem of greater population density.

Large societies of food producers have not only more members but also higher population densities than do small bands of hunter-gatherers. … As population densities increases, the territory of that band-sized population of a few dozen would shrink to a small area, with more and more of life’s necessities having to be obtained outside the area. … [S]patial realities require that densely population regions support large and complexly organized societies. {Diamond, 1998 #14} [pp. 287-8]
To some extent, all other problems derive from this problem. The only way of ensuring that the environment can sustain large numbers of people is to reshape the environment towards human needs. This, in essence, is what the growth of agriculture and animal husbandry represent, and the causes and effects of this transition are, more so than anything else, the main issues discussed within Guns, Germs and Steel.
Unlike hunter-gathering, where tribes can be nomadic, moving from place to place as particular habitats start to show the strain of sustaining human life, agriculture requires people become sedentary. The artificial environments people build around their own particular needs – which includes monocultural fields of wheat as much as cities and other urban landscapes -  exist only where people create them. These artificial environments allow people to survive at greater rates, and in larger numbers, but only when they are situated nearby. 

Initially, the problem of excess population density could be alleviated by expansion: sub-sections of the population move from the ‘urban’ centre to the ‘rural’ periphery. But if a higher density, ‘urban’, social group then decides it wants the land on which the ‘rural’ periphery currently live, then the force of numbers is on the side the larger group. 

And what happens when there is nowhere left for the groups, wishing to avoid the associated with higher density living, to go to?
As recently as A.D. 1500, less than 20 percent of the world’s land area was marked off by boundaries into states run by bureaucrats and governed by laws. Today, all land except Antarctica’s is so divided. {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 266]

The problem of conflict between unrelated strangers.

To many people in modern societies, to suggest this could be a major problem is to betray an unreasonably and unrealistically cynical view about the human race. However, there does not appear to be sufficient evidence to be convinced that the problems of anarchic, reciprocal, internecine – and overwhelmingly male-on-male – killing between rivals and strangers can easily be severe enough that large population densities cannot be maintained unless the problem is dealt with. 
There is no hard-and-fast limit to the number at which non-elite solutions to the problem of lethal conflict cease to be effective, however: 
“a few hundred” seems to be an upper limit for group size compatible with everyone’s knowing everybody. … One reason why the organization of human government tends to change from that of a tribe to that of a chiefdom in societies with more than a few hundred members is that the difficult issue of conflict resolution between strangers becomes increasingly acute in larger groups. A fact further diffusing potential problems of conflict resolution in tribes is that almost everyone is related to everyone else, by blood or marriage or both. Those ties of relationships binding all tribal members make police, laws, and other conflict-resolving institutions of larger societies unnecessary, since any two villagers getting into an argument will share many kin, who apply pressure on them to keep it from becoming violent.  {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 271]
However, the “problem grows astronomically as the number of people making up the society increases.” {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 286]
Relationships within a band of 20 people involve only 190 two-person interactions (20 people times 19 divided by 2), but a band of 2,000 people would have 1,999,000 dyads. Each of those dyads represents a potential time bomb that could explode in a murderous argument. Each murder in band and tribal societies usually leads to an attempted revenge killing, starting one more unending cycle of murder and countermurder that destabilizes the society. {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 286]
The ‘solution’ elites have ‘offered’ to this problem is Hobbesian: “for one person, the chief, to exercise a monopoly on the legitimate use of force.”{Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 273]
[The] chief held a recognized office, filled by hereditary right. Instead of the decentralized anarchy of a village meeting, the chief was a permanent centralized authority, made all significant decisions, and had a monopoly on critical information. {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p.273]
It is worth taking note, at this point, that the words ‘police’, ‘polity’, ‘policy’, ‘politic’, ‘political’ and ‘polite’ all share the same etymological lineage. 

The problem of transferring goods between members.

One of the primary advantages of any form of social group is the ability it provides its members to share risks. People are simply not utility-maximising robots, but instead risk-minimising, mortal, animals. The gain to one’s well-being of having twice as much food to eat as one needs is not twice as beneficial as having half as much food as one needs to eat is detrimental: the former provides some slight gluttonous pleasure; the latter brings the chronic, intense pain of starvation. Though one can calculate the expected return from an investment on average, what matters most to people is the minimisation of the probability that one’s return will ever fall below some minimal threshold: the threshold required for survival. In small groups, where everyone knows each other by name and reputation, sharing one’s surplus ensures that one’s own fortunes are always closely aligned to those of the group, and thus that, so long as the group’s resources per capita are above the starvation threshold, no one will starve. 
However, as the group size is small, it may be that none or very few of the members have been fortunate in acquiring resources, and thus bad fortune could mean the starvation of the entire group. Having a larger group means greater stability in the per capita wealth of the group; however, this larger size also leads to far greater logistical difficulties in ensuring effective distribution of such wealth to all members. 
As Diamond puts it:      
Any society requires means to transfer goods between its members. One individual may happen to acquire more of some essential commodity on one day and less on another. Because individuals have different talents, one individual consistently tends to wind up with an excess of some essentials and deficit of others. In small societies with a few pairs of members, the resulting necessary transfers of goods can be arranged directly between pairs of individuals or families, by reciprocal exchanges. But  the same mathematics that make direct pairwise conflict resolution inefficient in large societies makes direct pairwise economic transfers also inefficient. Large societies can function economically only if they have a redistributive economy in addition to a reciprocal economy. Goods in excess of an individual’s needs must be transferred from the individual to a centralized authority, which then redistributes the goods to individuals with deficits. {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 287]
As well as, and to an extent despite, the symbolic violence of the potlatch, the ceding of some proportion of one’s wealth to an administrative elite may be tolerated by commoners so long as the administrators ensure the availability of sufficient food on occasions when both oneself, and one’s friends, are simultaneously suffering from acute shortage. The establishment of the administrated redistributive economy, in parallel with the kinship-based reciprocal economy, ensures that members of larger social groups are able to access its risk-minimising benefits. 

The growing impossibility of communal decision making.

[Write something appropriate here…]
Elites, the Establishment, and Durable Inequality
[Introduction]
[C]hiefdoms introduced the dilemma fundamental to all centrally governed, nonegalitarian societies. At best , they do so by providing expensive services impossible to contract for on an individual basis. At worst, they function unabashedly as kleptocracies, transferring net wealth from commoners to upper classes. These noble and selfish functions are inextricably linked, although some governments emphasize much more of one function than of the other. The difference between a kleptocrat and a wise statesman, between a robber baron and a public benefactor, is merely one of degree: a matter of just how large a percentage of the tribute extracted from producers is retained by the elite, and how much the commoners like the public uses to which the redistributed tribute is put. {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 276]
[Write something]
What should an elite do to gain popular support while still maintaining a more comfortable lifestyle than commoners? Kleptocrats throughout the ages have resorted to a mixture of four solutions:

Disarm the populace, and arm the elite. […]
Make the masses happy by redistributing much of the tribute received, in popular ways. […]
Use the monopoly of force to promote happiness, by maintaining public order and curbing violence. […]
[C]onstruct an ideology or religion justifying kleptocracy {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 277]

[Write something]
When supernatural beliefs gained [the function of serving to justify central authority, justify transfer of wealth, or maintaining peace between unrelated individuals] and became institutionalized, they were transformed into what we term a religion. {Diamond, 1998 #14}[pp. 276-7]
Diamond Quotes
A prerequisite for living in settlements is either food production or else a productive environment with especially concentrated resources that can be hunted and gathered within a small area.{Diamond, 1998 #14} [p. 271]
Tribes … share with bands an “egalitarian” social system, without ranked lineages or classes. Not only is status not inherited; no member of a traditional tribe or band can become disproportionately wealthier by his or her own efforts, because each individual has debts and obligations to many others.{Diamond, 1998 #14} [p.272]
As regards population size, chiefdoms were considerably larger than tribes, ranging from several thousand to several tens of thousands of people. That size created serious potential for internal conflict because, for any person living in a chiefdom, the vast majority of other people in the chiefdom were neither closely related by blood or marriage nor known by name. With the rise of chiefdoms around 7,500 years ago, people had to learn,  for the first time in history, how to encounter strangers regularly without attempting to kill them.{Diamond, 1998 #14}
 [p.273]
[W]hy do the commoners tolerate the transfer of the fruits of their hard labor to kleptocrats? This question, raised by political theorists from Plato to Marx, is raised anew by voters in every modern election. Kleptocracies with little public support run the risk of being overthrown, wither by downtrodden commoners or by upstart would-be replacement kleptocrats seeking public support by promising a higher ratio of services rendered to fruits stolen. {Diamond, 1998 #14} [p. 276]
When a large portion of the goods received from commoners  was not redistributed to them but was retained and consumed by the chiefly lineages and craftspeople, the redistribution became tribute, a precursor of taxes that made its first appearance in chiefdoms. From the commoners the chiefs claimed not only goods but also labor for construction of public works, which again might return to benefit the commoners (for example, irrigation systems to help feed everybody) or instead benefited mainly the chiefs (for instance, lavish tombs). {Diamond, 1998 #14}[p. 275]

Source: Pages 1–16 of MintonThesis.pdf (September 2009). Text extracted from PDF; figures extracted directly as images.

University of York
Work and its Other: A Multipleperspective Analysis of Incapacity Benefit Reform
Jonathan William Minton Department of Sociology July 2009
 Abstract
This is a thesis of two halves: five „theory‟ chapters and five „empirical‟ chapters, prefaced by an introduction, and then followed by a conclusion. The five „empirical‟ chapters are based around understanding the development and rollout of a programme of Incapacity Benefits reform known as „Pathways to Work‟, which was first introduced in 2002. Pathways to Work has since been implemented in all Jobcentre Plus offices across the UK, and so this half of the thesis could be seen as a case study of a „successfully implemented‟ government policy. The five „theoretical‟ chapters introduce, explain, and interpret a range of theories about social processes that operate at various temporal and spatial scales. These theories range from the very small scale (human neurobiology) to the very large scale (archaeology and biogeography), and conclude with mid-scale theories relating to observed and intuitive everyday interpersonal interactions. The two halves of the thesis are intended to be complementary, with the „theoretical‟ half illuminating the „empirical‟ half and the „empirical‟ half illuminating the „theoretical‟ half. The thesis concludes by offering a number of policy suggestions, and making the complementarity between thesis halves more explicit.

 Acknowledgements Progress towards completing this thesis has been approximately sigmoid in shape: slow in the first year, increasingly fast in the second year (“I think you‟re about six months from completion…”), and then slow again in the third year (“…maybe not.”). So slow, in fact, that the third year stretched over into a fourth year as, well, „other stuff happened‟. Sigmoid curves are asymptotic, never quite reaching their destination. I want to apologise to my thesis advisory „team‟ – Roger Burrows, Danny Dorling, and Kate Pickett – for at times making the sigmoid curve look more like prophecy than approximation. I also want to thank Roger and Danny for allowing me the opportunity to begin the PhD in the first place, and for then allowing it to develop (mutate?) from a GIS project to something closer to a work of applied political philosophy. I would also like to thank Stuart Adam, Jenny Allen, Kelly Benneworth, Beverly Geesin, Roger Bowles, Jonathan Bradshaw, Gill Callaghan, Colin Campbell, Stephen Clayton, Thomas Cochrane, Jacqueline Davidson, Richard Dorsett, Paul Drew, JeanBaptiste Gouyon, Steve Hall, Martin Ham, Nicola Holt, Gary King, Richard King, Graham Lewis, Daryl Martin, Duncan McVicar, Rob Mellows, Dan Mercea, Andrew Oswald, Theo Papadopoulos, Simon Parker, Nicholas Pleace, Darren Reed, Barbara Robertson, Roy Sainsbury, Simon Shaw, Phil Stanworth, Charles Tilly, Merran Toerien, Emma Uprichard, Roberto de Vogli, Simon Walker, Gemma Warren, Richard Wilkinson, Astrid Winkler, and Simon Winlow. Each of these people, I think, has contributed towards my thinking about the concepts, facts, and ideas developed over the course of the PhD. They, of course, deserve some of the credit but none of the blame for what is presented here, which they may disagree with entirely. Finally, I would like to thank my mother, Helen Minton, for eagle-eyed proofreading, and to her and my father, Nigel Minton, for allowing me to soliloquise at length to them about many of the ideas contained within this thesis, even where such soliloquies were unsolicited; and to thank Natalie Wheatley, also for proof-reading, and for offering persistent encouragement that I “complete and submit it”, despite not really knowing what „it‟ is.

title: Academic Works
text:
Before becoming a statistician and data scientist, I pursued an unusual academic path: from Electronic Engineering through Critical Theory to Sociology and Human Geography. These three works trace that intellectual journey.
Each work is presented here as a navigable web document, with the original files available for download where possible. The content has been recovered from CD-ROM and DVD backups spanning 2003–2011, converted from Word documents to this format.

title: Academic Works
text:
Technical notes
These documents were recovered from personal CD-ROM and DVD backups dating from 2003 to 2011. The original files were Microsoft Word documents (.doc/.docx), converted to markdown using textutil (macOS) and Python processing. Some content was lost in conversion:

Figures and diagrams: The BEng thesis referenced CorelDRAW graphics and MATLAB figures; these are shown as placeholders in the web version but the original image files (430+ BMP/EMF) are preserved in the archive.
Equations: Mathematical notation embedded via Microsoft Equation Editor was stripped during conversion; equation references are preserved as placeholders.
The MA thesis is a working draft with visible authorial notes — this is presented as-is, preserving the texture of work in progress.
The PhD is presented in two versions: the 2008 working draft (pre-revision) and the final submitted PDF (2009).

The underlying archive, including all original MATLAB code, test images, and document files, is maintained in a private vault. The MATLAB codebase for the BEng project is being ported to Python for interactive demonstration.

title: Abstract
text:
The purpose of this project is to create a working prototype for a machine vision system capable of being used in certain types of machine calibration and related problems.
Specifically, the prototype needs to be able to identify and determine the real-life displacement of an object within fixed boundaries on a two-dimensional plane, and provide information to the prototype user.

Figures created in CorelDRAW and MATLAB are shown as placeholders; the original image files (430+ bitmaps, 59 vector graphics) and complete MATLAB codebase (175+ source files) are preserved in the archive.

title: BEng: An Investigation Into Machine Vision Calibration Systems
text:
Abstract
The purpose of this project is to create a working prototype for a machine vision system capable of being used in certain types of machine calibration and related problems. Specifically, the prototype needs to be able to identify and determine the real-life displacement of an object within fixed boundaries on a two-dimensional plane, and provide information to the prototype user.
crumbs: BEng: An Investigation Into Machine Vision Calibration Systems

title: BEng: An Investigation Into Machine Vision Calibration Systems
text:
Key results
The system achieved:

0.01% accuracy relative to the bounded area for object displacement measurement
Successful single-camera operation (improved over original two-camera design)
0.5mm precision in real-world displacement measurement (actual: 6.0mm right, 4.0mm down; measured: 5.5mm, 4.0mm)
Robust performance across varying lighting conditions and with non-critical objects present
crumbs: BEng: An Investigation Into Machine Vision Calibration Systems

title: BEng: An Investigation Into Machine Vision Calibration Systems
text:
Technical approach
The project combined two complementary techniques:

Digital Image Correlation (DIC) — using 2D fast Fourier transforms to detect displacement between image pairs
Fiduciary Marker Analysis — using colour-based detection with edge finding, dilation, hole-filling, and erosion to identify reference points

The system operated in two stages:

Stage 1 (Prototype Positioning): Detecting and correcting camera-to-plane misalignment using fiduciary markers
Stage 2 (Object Displacement): Using DIC to find the actual object displacement, with rigid-body compensation

title: Methods and Process
text:
4
 Methods and Process
3.1 Section Introduction
This section considers the processes required for the prototype system to deliver the required results. This involves both a schematic description- involving inputs, processes and outputs- and a more detailed description of each of these processes.
Overall Process
3.2.1 Introduction
The prototype must allow the user to identify what the object displacement is from its current location, and that on a stored (archived) image. For this, it needs to know the displacement of the object, in the plane, and in a real-life quantity such as millimetres.
As stated previously (Section 1.3.1), the problem splits broadly into three tasks:
Locating the plane: Positioning the prototype device correctly relative to the object plane.
Locating the object: Finding the position of the object within the fixed boundaries of the area on the plane.
Finding object displacement: Finding how far the object has moved in the plane from its original position.
Tasks 2 and 3 can be handled at the same time: One first has to locate the object in order to determine its disparity, and thus any object image created in task 2 can subsequently be used to determine object displacement. Thus tasks 2 and 3 are combined together, in the code, into what is called Stage 2.
Whereas tasks 2 and 3 are best combined, the task of positioning the prototype device correctly relative to the plane  (Task 1) is best handled if broken down into a number of sub-stages.
Stage 1: Prototype Positioning
Ideally, the camera should be positioned such that it faces the bounded area of the plane directly; and at a distance from the plane far enough that all of the marker features are in view, but near enough that the pixel resolution of the camera device does not greatly impair the ability for the device to determine small degrees of object disparity (as the distance increases, so does the area of real space represented by a single pixel).
When dealing with a simple rectangular pattern of features, as in the test case, the camera should ideally be positioned such that the features are as follows: [Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]
Ideal Image Position
[Fig 3.2.1]
One could perform a very basic position detection system by attempting to determine that the centre of the features is at the centre of the image, and that the position of the features in the image are equidistant from this central point; but this would only tell the user whether the camera is positioned correctly, or not correctly. To aid the user in positioning the device, the system needs to be able to tell the user both whether the camera is positioned correctly or not, and – if not correctly aligned – how the image is mis-positioned, and what action the user should take to rectify this.
There are a number of specific ways that the camera could be mis-positioned. It could be: parallel to the plane but rotated slightly in this plane (1); rotated slightly in the ‘vertical’ plane perpendicular to that of the object plane (2); or rotated slightly in the ‘horizontal’ plane perpendicular to that of the object plane (3): [Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]
[Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]
[Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]
(1) (2) (3)
[Fig. 3.2.2]
The actual skew of the image is likely to be a combination of these three types of rotation: [Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]
Combination of skews
[Fig. 3.2.3]
Aligning the camera can be handed as a three-stage process. For demonstration purposes, the prototype only works to determine type (1) rotation, and then type (3) rotation. Type (2) rotation is analogous to type (3) rotation, and so showing that the method works for type (3) rotation means that type (2) problems can also be solved.
Thus, the process used to determine whether the camera is correctly positioned assumes the camera is first positioned correctly vertically: that it is at the correct height relative to the plane, and not at an angle. 
Assuming this, the code first corrects type (1) rotation (the Main 1a function), then type (3) rotation (The Main 1b function). A full system would include a third function to determine type (2) rotation, but the process for this is analogous to correcting type (3) rotation, and so the system does not have to demonstrate this in order to demonstrate its effectiveness.
Stage 2: Determining Object Displacement
Once correctly positioned, the next stage is to use digital image correlation (DIC) methods to determine the displacement between the current object position, and that in an archived image. 
Performing a straight DIC comparison of the two images (the current image, and the archive one where the object is correctly positioned) is not an effective way of determining object displacement. A DIC output of a full image may suggest a ‘match’ at a number of positions, for a number of reasons. If there is a repeated pattern on the image plane then the DIC method will match each repeated feature in the pattern in one image, to both the equivalent and the adjacent repeated features in the other image. 
Although Stage 1 of the process ensures the camera is at the right angle relative to the object area, it does not ensure the camera is in a completely central position regarding shifts in the direction of the plane. The camera position of the current image may be displaced horizontally and vertically. To ensure that this form of displacement, known as rigid body displacement, does not get confused by the computer with object displacement, DIC is performed on the marker masks of both the archive and the current images. This produces an unambiguous figure for this rigid body displacement. Using this, versions of the two images – current and archive – are created that compare only the region of the images that are common to both, and in the object intensity plane.
A DIC is then performed of these two adjusted object images, and the displacement figure given by finding the position of the greatest intensity in the DIC output used as the object displacement in pixels.
To convert the displacement in pixels to a displacement in millimetres (or another real unit), a conversion scale is created. This involves finding the distance in pixels between markers, and comparing them with the values given for distance between the same markers as part of the image geometry information given at the start. In the test example- four markers marking out the corners of a rectangle- the distances between the centres of the two ‘rows’ and ‘columns’ of markers are compared with the height and width of the rectangle given in the marker geometry information.
Flow Diagrams
The following pages provide flow diagrams of the Matlab program written for this project. Further details on the implementation of these processes are provided via the example case in the next section (4.2.1), which shows how a single test case is analysed and interpreted by each consecutive stage. 
One may find the test example more useful than the following process schematics when first considering how the prototype system works, and the schematics useful in determining how the system operates in the general case. 
In attempting to understand the actual techniques used to create markers and determine their centre positions, one should refer to sections 2.3 and 4.2.2.1 for explanations of the theory behind and examples resulting from this.
Black curved rectangles indicate overall start and end processes. Red curved rectangles indicate intermediate (function level) start and end processes. Green diamonds indicate decision nodes. Normal rectangles indicate self-contained processes. Grey rectangles with triangles within indicate internal data acquisition stages (Bracketed entries indicate optional data). Irregular parallelograms indicate external inputs (such as camera image acquisition) if bold, and external outputs (feedback to user) if italic.
Main Function [Diagram — original PowerPoint file not available in web version]
“C:\My Documents\Final year Project\FLOW1.PPT” “”   * MERGEFORMAT 
The Main Function: This function loads in the real geometry of the markers, an image of the object in the area when both the camera and object are correctly positioned, and the colour scheme used for both the Marker and the Object. See section 4.2 for an example of this function using a test case
[Fig. 3.3.1]
Main 1a Function [Diagram — original PowerPoint file not available in web version]
“C:\My Documents\Final year Project\FLOW1A.PPT” “”   * MERGEFORMAT 
Main 1a (The first plane positioning function): * This position locates the positions of the markers, finds the angle between the larger of the two vertical sidebands and the vertical, and reports this to the user if it is not vertical (Solves type (1) rotation). See section 4.2.2.2 for an example of this function*
[Fig. 3.3.2]
Main 1b Function [Diagram — original PowerPoint file not available in web version]
“C:\My Documents\Final year Project\FLOW1B.PPT” “”   * MERGEFORMAT 
Main 1b (The second plane positioning function): * This function locates the positions of the markers, finds the angle between the larger of the two vertical sidebands and the vertical, and reports this to the user if it is not vertical (Solves Type (3) rotation). See section 4.2.3 for an example of this function*
[Fig. 3.3.3]**
Main 2 Function [Figure — original PowerPoint diagram not available in web version]
Main 2: * This function finds the real object displacement, compared to the archive position, and notifies the user and repeats if the object is not in this original position. This stage combines both rigid body disparity determination and object disparity determination (stage 2), and conversion of object disparity figures into real-life values (stage 2) into a single function. See section 4.2.4 for an example of this function*
[Fig. 3.3.4]**
Find Object Disparity Function [Diagram — original PowerPoint file not available in web version]
“C:\My Documents\Final year Project\DISPMAIN.PPT” “”   * MERGEFORMAT 
Find Object Disparity: * This function performs a digital image correlation of the two masks (current and archive) to determine rigid body disparity, then uses this rigid body disparity figure to create rigid body disparity-adjusted images of the objects, to determine object the object disparity in pixels. See 4.2.4.2-4 for an example of this function*
[Fig. 3.3.5]**
Get Marker Coordinates Function [Diagram — original PowerPoint file not available in web version]
“C:\My Documents\Final year Project\MARKERCOORDS.PPT” “”   * MERGEFORMAT 
Get Marker Coordinates: * This function uses information on the marker colour scheme (and the techniques discussed in section 2.3.3) to create a one-bit mask of the marker. As the test shape is of a rectangle, it then converts this single mask into masks of the four quarters, and finds the centres of each the marker objects in each. From this, it determines the position of the centres of the markers in the overall mask, and thus the image. See section 4.2.2.1 for an example of this function*
[Fig. 3.3.6]

title: Introduction
text:
1
Introduction
1.1 Overall Introduction
The overall aim of this project is to create a functioning prototype for device capable of determining the displacement of an ‘object,’ within a two-dimensional plane, between its current position, and its position at a previous time: [Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]
The Problem: A prototype device needs to be able to know how much an object has moved in a two-dimensional plane from a previous occasion
[Fig. 1.1]
The prototype should be able to report to the user how much the object is displaced from its original position, and thus allow the user to adjust the re-position the object into its original location
1.2 Examples
Introduction
Before continuing, it is useful to consider some real-life scenarios where finding a solution to the problem described is useful.
1.2.2 A Specific Example
Consider a machine designed to solder a component on a circuit board. The machine has a circuit holder, and under which a tool head – holding the soldering device –, moved by two pairs of motors normal to each other, so that the tool head moves in the same plane as the circuit. After finishing soldering a component, the tool head returns to its rest position. If the actual rest position of the tool head is significantly different to the calibrated one, then the soldering device will solder in the wrong position and the component will not be soldered correctly:
[Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]
A Specific Example: The tool head moves within a two-dimensional plane. When it has finished machining a part, it returns to a rest position. If the rest position it actually returns to is different to the intended rest position, then all machining operations by the device will be in the wrong place.
[Fig. 1.2]
To calibrate using the calibrating device:
The four extremities of the rectangular circuit holder could be marked with a colour used nowhere else on or near the machine (such as with a fluorescent colour); the soldering device could be removed, leaving the tool head, which could be marked at a specific position with another distinct, but different, colour.
The camera could be positioned, by hand, roughly above machine, such that four rectangular fixed points on the soldering device (and thus the points marking the machine geometry) are visible to the calibrating device. An image could then be taken, and the position of these machine geometry points determined. If the shape, formed by the points, matches the geometry of points defining the machine input previously into the calibrating device, then the calibrating device is known to be at the correct angle to the machine. 
Using existing digital image correlation (DIC) techniques, any disparity between a pair of images, where the disparity is local to part of the image-pair, can be determined, and distinguished between disparity common throughout the image-pair (Which is known as ‘rigid-body’ disparity, and may occur, for example, if one of the images is taken when one image is taken further to the right than the other image). This local disparity, common only to a small portion of the image-image pair, indicates that something within the machine, and not the machine itself nor the camera, has moved – Thus, hopefully, the tool head disparity has been identified. 
From this information, the actual position of the tool had can be determined and, as before, compared with the desired position, and the disparity output to the user as a real value.
1.2.3 Some Other Examples
Other examples of real-life problems that require this kind of object displacement within a two-dimensional plane to be identified are as follows:
Quality Control: When a circuit board has been made, an image of the board could be taken, and the position of the components on the board compared with those taken on a correctly positioned test image. If any of the components are significantly out of position then the board could be rejected and the machinery that places the misaligned components investigated.
Robot Tool-head Positioning: Using a pair of the devices, placed at different positions, the position of a tool-head of a robot, or other machine whose field of movement in three-dimensions, could be compared with that when properly in positions. Finding the displacement of an object in three dimensions can be solved as a set of Cartesian problems involving displacement in two-dimensional planes.
Abstraction Of Problem
Introduction
The problem to be solved could be considered analytically as involving three stages:
Locating the object plane: The prototype device needs to be able to determine its own position relative to the plane the object exists on.
Locating the object: The prototype device needs to be able to locate the position of the object within the plane.
Finding object displacement: The prototype device needs to be able to determine the displacement in the plane of the object, between its current position, and the correct position.
Each stage of the problem will now be described individually:
Stage One: Locating The Plane
Consider a three-dimensional space: Within the space is a two dimensional plane and the calibrating device. The calibrating device could be thought considered as a point Pcam, and a unit vector Vcam, located at point Pcam. The point Pcam marks the camera position, and the unit vector Vcam marks the direction the camera faces.
On the plane is a bounded area A, at the centre of which is a point Pplane, from which a unit vector Vnormal, of the normal to the plane, could be located.  
Starting at Pcam, one could consider a viewing cone, in direction Vcam, to emanate. 
The camera should be positioned so that the bounded area A lies within the viewing cone. 
Certain features of the bounded area A should be identified. With these, the direction of the normal vector of the plane Vnormal, and the position of point Pplane, should be identified.
With this information, the camera should be positioned such that Vnormal = -Vcam, and that both Pplane and Pcam lie at some position within this vector: i.e., that the camera directly faces the area of interest.
The process for positioning the camera with respect to the plane could itself be completed in a number of stages: First to locate the whole of the whole of the area A, then to ensure the camera faces the plane normally in the x-y axis (for example), then the y-z axis. 
Stage Two: Locating The Object
Assuming the camera is now facing the area of interest, the next stage is to locate the position of the object within the bounded area A. This could be considered as follows:
An object, O, exists somewhere within the fixed boundaries of the area on the two-dimensional plane. 
A certain feature marking part of the area boundary is located and considered the origin point (0,0) of the area. Other features of the area boundary are identified, and used later. [Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]

The object of interest, existing within the area, is identified, and its position in the camera image is found relative to the origin feature: [Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]

1.3.4 Stage Three: Finding Object Disparity
Having located the current object position, it now has to be compared with its original position, and the displacement between these two positioned provided to the user. There are, broadly, two ways to do this:
The position of the movable object in the image at the present moment could be translated into a real position (in millimetres, centimetres etc) by comparing its position to those of fixed marker objects (the identified boundary features) whose real geometries (the real-life distances and angles between objects) are known. This could be compared with the real-life position of the movable object within the area when correctly positioned, and the displacement between these two positions output to the user. equation [Eqn. 1.3.2]
An image of the object in the area, when in the correct position, could be stored in the prototype system. This could be compared directly with the image of the object in the area at the present moment, and the disparity between the two objects in the two images (in pixels) determined. This disparity in pixels could then be translated into a real-life disparity by comparing the distances of the fixed boundary features in the images, with their actual distances between these fixed features. Again, this essentially provides a scale, which can be used to convert between pixel disparities and real-life disparities. [Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]
An image of the object when in the correct position
[Fig. 1.3.3] equation [Eqn. 1.3.3]
The second approach is used in the project, because using this approach means:
The user does not actually have to know the real-life position of the object when in the correct position, just have taken an image of it at this position to cross-reference with.
The marker does not have to be painted or connected to something of a specific colour or shape for the prototype to identify, which would be difficult to arrange if the object is an intricate part of a machine. 
The Test Problem
For the purpose of testing the prototype in this project, the bounded area used was of a simple rectangle, the boundary features being the four corners. The distances between the centres of the boundary features (i.e. the height and width of the rectangle) were set and known by the prototype system for referencing with the position of these features in images: [Figure — original CorelDRAW graphic not available in web version]

1.5 Possible Techniques To Solving The Problem
*** 1.5.1 Introduction***
Two basic techniques were considered to solve the test problem: Digital image correlation (DIC) based on fast-Fourier transforms (FFTs), and fiduciary marker analysis:
*** 1.5.2 Digital Image Correlation***
Digital image correlation is based on two-dimensional fast Fourier transforms (FFTs). It allows two similar images to be compared to locate areas of disparity between the two images as a whole, or sub-areas of the two images.
Presently, DIC techniques have been used and developed by the university to provide highly accurate and comparatively inexpensive methods to determine minute relative displacements in objects due to known and controlled variables (such as the physical warping of microchips due to heat generated during operation) under controlled (laboratory) conditions. 
Applying DIC techniques towards this end is a potentially inexpensive alternative to developing ever more intricate cameras, as it relies more heavily on software than hardware: For example, sub-pixel algorithm techniques have been developed by the university to allow much more displacement information to be acquired from the same physical device, and thus allow more accurate data to be acquired at the same material expense. 
In laboratory conditions, the position of the imaging device relative to the two-dimensional plane on which the image-pair disparity occurs can be controlled and known to a high degree. In the workplace, this is not the case, and so the imaging device needs be able to have some means of sensing its own position relative to the plane of interest. DIC techniques exist that allow this plane positioning information to be determined, but the most obvious application for DIC techniques is determining the position of the object within the plane, when the imaging device is facing the plane directly. 
A more detailed explanation of the theory behind DIC is given in the next section (Section 2.2).
1.5.3 Fiduciary Marker Analysis
An imaging device is capable of distinguishing between objects within the image if they have distinctive properties. They can have a size, shape, or colour that distinguishes them from other parts of the image. If the objects within the image can be reliably distinguished from others they can be used as fiduciary markers, to be used as reference points to determine displacement, scale and position of both objects and the imaging device:
If the relative positions of a number of distinguishable markers are fixed and the position of a camera is variable, they can be used to determine the position of the camera relative to the markers. 
If the positions of the markers vary, but the camera position is fixed, they can be used to determine the real displacements of objects and parts on which the markers exist.
Brief Discussion Of Approaches
Both DIC and fiduciary marker analysis can be applied to solving all three areas of the problem. Each has distinct advantages and disadvantages when applied to different types of task, due to the way they work:
